Brookline Select Board Meeting - March 10, 2026

Select Board · Brookline · 2026-03-10
tap to load the tape · nothing plays until you do
AI summary — supplements the official record

During the Brookline Select Board meeting on March 10, 2026, various topics were discussed including preparations for upcoming events, community development projects, and budget concerns related to housing and public services. There was a notable focus on efforts to modernize the green line program and improve accessibility at train stations. The board also reviewed grant proposals and the implications of potential zoning changes in the Chestnut Hill commercial area. Additionally, the town discussed plans to enhance community engagement in budgetary matters and streamlined communications with residents about town projects. The meeting concluded with plans to continue these discussions in future sessions. For further details, timestamps range from [17:35] to [422:54] in the transcript.

1:14:25 Brookline Klein got this. Um, on

1:14:29 Saturday on was it Sunday?

1:14:32 Was it Saturday or Sunday? Anyway, this

1:14:35 weekend uh I represented the town of

1:14:38 Brooklyn at a Boston 250 event at the uh

1:14:41 Unitarian Universalist uh urban ministry

1:14:45 in Roxbury. Uh it was a really nice

1:14:48 program commemorating the uh uh 1776

1:14:54 um effort by uh Henry Knox who you may

1:14:58 know of as the person that Fort Knox was

1:15:01 named after. Uh used to be Secretary of

1:15:04 Treasury. Uh but in 1776 he took a band

1:15:08 of folks revolutionaries to Fort

1:15:11 Ticonderoga to to to uh recover cannons

1:15:16 and other ordinance that the British had

1:15:18 abandoned and brought them I think it's

1:15:21 like 300 miles in the dead of winter um

1:15:25 over um difficult terrain without modern

1:15:29 highways of course um and over frozen

1:15:33 lakes and and rivers.

1:15:35 to Cambridge and then Roxbury and uh

1:15:39 Dorchester. And those cannons were used

1:15:42 to repel the British and and drive them

1:15:45 out of uh uh Boston. And as a result, we

1:15:49 have the Massachusetts holiday called

1:15:51 Evacuation Day, uh repres uh

1:15:54 commemorating the evacuation of Boston

1:15:56 by the British. Now, what does Brooklyn

1:15:58 have to do with that? Well, they came

1:16:02 whatever route they came to Cambridge

1:16:04 and to get to Roxbury without running

1:16:08 into the British, they had to go back to

1:16:09 Watertown and through Brooklyn into

1:16:12 Roxbury. So, because we were a stop on

1:16:16 on this uh trip or or we uh they had to

1:16:20 go through Brooklyn uh to get to their

1:16:23 their ult their um final destination. We

1:16:28 are recognized as having played an

1:16:31 important part in this very important um

1:16:36 um event that was uh very significant in

1:16:41 the American Revolution. Without running

1:16:44 the British out of Boston, things would

1:16:46 have been much much different to the

1:16:49 worst for for the Americans.

1:16:52 Thirdly, so that that's the plaque that

1:16:56 we have

1:16:58 and we'll find some place probably in

1:30:10 So, in in in order to welcome them to

1:30:13 Brooklyn, uh we prepared a proclamation

1:30:17 and I'd like to read the proclamation.

1:30:19 It says proclamation of the Brooklyn

1:30:21 Select Board. The Brooklyn Select Board

1:30:24 welcomes Mayor Seamus Ryan of Waterford,

1:30:27 Ireland, and Mayor Mike Cubert of the of

1:30:30 Goway, Ireland. Oh, thank you. Oops.

1:30:34 Okay. For St. Patrick's Day celebrations

1:30:38 and events

1:30:41 and I have a Irish welcome statement and

1:30:47 I don't have the pronunciation right in

1:30:50 front of me so I'm going to reme try to

1:30:52 remember

1:30:54 yes right mele f which means a 100,000

1:31:01 welcomes so whereas the month of March

1:31:04 is designated as Irish American American

1:31:06 Heritage Month, a time to honor the

1:31:08 indelible contributions, vibrant

1:31:11 culture, and enduring spirit of Irish

1:31:13 immigrants and their descendants who

1:31:15 have shaped the fabric of American life.

1:31:19 And whereas the greater Boston area

1:31:21 proudly holds one of the nation's

1:31:23 deepest concentrations of Irish

1:31:25 heritage, a legacy rooted in the 19th

1:31:28 century when many sought refuge in

1:31:30 Brooklyn following the great Irish

1:31:32 famine. And whereas though these early

1:31:36 residents faced significant adversity

1:31:38 and hardships, they met every challenge

1:31:41 with resilience and faith, eventually

1:31:44 rising to positions of political and

1:31:46 economic leadership and integrating

1:31:48 their traditions into the very soul of

1:31:50 our town. And whereas the legacy is

1:31:53 anchored by enduring institutions such

1:31:55 as St. Mary of the Assumption Catholic

1:31:57 Church, which has served as a spiritual

1:32:00 and communal pillar for generations of

1:32:03 Brooklyn families. And whereas Brooklyn

1:32:05 remains a global center for Irish

1:32:08 scholarship through Boston College's

1:32:10 Center for Irish programs and Boston

1:32:13 University's Institute for the Study of

1:32:15 Irish Culture, ensuring that the history

1:32:17 and contemporary issues of Ireland

1:32:19 continue to be studied and celebrated.

1:32:22 And whereas the town of Brooklyn was the

1:32:24 childhood home of John Fitzgerald

1:32:27 Kennedy, the 35th president of the

1:32:29 United States, whose life and leadership

1:32:33 uh started at 83 Beal Street and remains

1:32:37 a powerful symbol of Irish-American, the

1:32:40 Irish-American journey from immigrant

1:32:42 roots to the highest office of the land.

1:32:44 Whereas the spirit of Irish hospitality

1:32:47 continues to thrive in our local

1:32:49 gathering places including grano meles

1:32:52 and Hamiltons which serve as modern-day

1:32:55 her hearths where stories are shared and

1:32:57 community bonds are strengthened. And

1:33:00 whereas on March 12, we are privileged

1:33:02 to extend a warm Brookline welcome to

1:33:04 Mayor Seamus Ryan of Waterford and Mayor

1:33:07 Mike Cubard of Goway, whose visit

1:33:10 reinforces the living bridge between our

1:33:13 town and

1:33:16 the ancestral homelands of many of our

1:33:19 residents. Now therefore, in

1:33:21 consideration of the foregoing on this

1:33:23 10th day of March, 2026, the Brookline

1:33:26 Select Board on behalf of the residents

1:33:28 of Brooklyn hereby welcomes Mayor Seamus

1:33:32 Ryan of Waterford and Mike Cupard of

1:33:35 Goway. And if we may use the Irish Gaic

1:33:39 phrase, we say kad mele f a 100,000

1:33:45 welcomes to our friends from Gowway and

1:33:47 Water for uh to be signed by the

1:33:49 Brookline Select Board. So

1:33:53 any other comments? I uh Michael.

1:33:56 Sure.

1:33:57 Um so uh I also have a proclamation to

1:34:01 read uh on behalf of the Brooklyn Select

1:34:04 Board. Uh, the Brooklyn Select Board

1:34:07 recognizes and congratulates the 2026

1:34:11 Brooklyn Women of the Year, Gabrielle

1:34:15 Abby Dean, Alicia Su, and Linda Olsen

1:34:20 Pelky.

1:34:22 Whereas the United Nations officially

1:34:24 recognized March 8th as International

1:34:26 Women's Day in 1975

1:34:29 and since 1977

1:34:32 has invited member states to proclaim

1:34:34 March 8th as the United Nations Day for

1:34:37 Women's Rights and World Peace, honoring

1:34:40 the historic and ongoing struggles of

1:34:42 women around the world for equality,

1:34:45 justice, and opportunity. And whereas

1:34:48 the Brooklyn Commission for Women was

1:34:50 established by Brooklyn Town Meeting

1:34:52 with a mission to promote equity and

1:34:55 justice for all women and girls,

1:34:57 including all who identify as female.

1:35:01 And whereas for 21 years, the Brooklyn

1:35:04 Commission for Women has recognized

1:35:06 outstanding women in our community

1:35:08 through its annual Brooklyn Women of the

1:35:11 Year Award, honoring women who live and

1:35:14 work in Brooklyn and who have dedicated

1:35:16 their time, resources, and efforts to

1:35:19 bringing significant and positive impact

1:35:21 to our community. And whereas the

1:35:24 Brooklyn Commission for Women has

1:35:26 selected longtime Brooklyn residents

1:35:28 Abby Dean, Alicia Su, and Linda Olsen

1:35:32 Pelky as the 2026 Brooklyn Women of the

1:35:36 Year. And whereas Abby Dean is a deeply

1:35:40 respected public health leader and

1:35:42 licensed clinical social worker who has

1:35:45 devoted more than 30 years to Brooklyn

1:35:47 in her current role as the behavioral

1:35:50 health intervention and prevention

1:35:52 program coordinator for Brooklyn Public

1:35:54 Health and as a counselor at Brooklyn

1:35:57 High School. She provides critical

1:35:59 mental health support to students,

1:36:02 staff, families while mentoring the next

1:36:05 generation of professionals. And whereas

1:36:08 through her statewide leadership on the

1:36:10 opioid recovery and remediation fund

1:36:12 board and her direction of programs

1:36:15 supporting mothers and children impacted

1:36:18 by addiction, she exemplifies

1:36:21 compassionate leadership and an

1:36:23 unwavering dedication to prevention,

1:36:26 equity, and the well-being of the entire

1:36:28 community. And whereas Alicia Su is a

1:36:32 retired teacher of the public schools of

1:36:34 Brooklyn who spent three decades

1:36:37 teaching primarily at the Amos A.

1:36:39 Lawrence School and was awarded the

1:36:42 Earnest R Cav Cavverly Award for

1:36:45 Excellence in Teaching from the Brooklyn

1:36:48 Education Foundation in 2008. And

1:36:51 whereas Alicia Shu is a co-founder and

1:36:54 steering committee member of the

1:36:56 Brooklyn Asian-American Family Network

1:36:58 and is a member of the planning

1:37:00 committee with the Office of Diversity,

1:37:02 Equity, Inclusion, and Community

1:37:04 Relations for the Town of Brooklyn,

1:37:07 where she co-leads the annual AAPI

1:37:10 Heritage Month Festival Celebration in

1:37:13 May. And whereas Alicia Shu is a has

1:37:17 been a tireless advocate for the

1:37:19 Asian-American and Pacific Islander

1:37:21 community in Brooklyn and is widely

1:37:24 recognized as a fierce advocate and

1:37:26 loving supporter of educators.

1:37:29 And whereas Linda Olsen Pelky is an

1:37:31 urban planner, civic leader, and

1:37:33 longtime volunteer whose work has had a

1:37:36 lasting impact on the town of Brookline

1:37:39 over more than two decades of public

1:37:41 service focused on planning

1:37:43 sustainability, open space, and

1:37:46 neighborhood engagement. And whereas

1:37:48 Linda Olsen Pelky has served as a town

1:37:50 meeting member since 2009,

1:37:53 currently serves on the advisory

1:37:55 committee and has served on several

1:37:57 other town committees. And whereas Linda

1:37:59 Olsen Pelky is the founder and president

1:38:02 of the Friends of the Mino Rose Garden,

1:38:05 serves on the board of the Brooklyn

1:38:07 Greenspace Alliance, previously served

1:38:09 on the board of the Brooklyn Community

1:38:11 Foundation, and is the author of

1:38:14 Exploring the Paths of Brooklyn, a guide

1:38:16 to the town's historic pedestrian paths.

1:38:19 And whereas through her leadership,

1:38:21 scholarship, and tireless volunteer

1:38:24 service, Linda Olsen Pelky has made a

1:38:26 significant and enduring contribution to

1:38:29 Brooklyn civic life. Now, therefore, in

1:38:33 consideration of the foregoing, on this

1:38:36 10th day of March, 2026, the Brooklyn

1:38:39 Select Board on behalf of the town of

1:38:41 Brooklyn hereby recognizes,

1:38:43 congratulates, and expresses its deepest

1:38:46 appreciation to Gabrielle Abby Dean,

1:38:50 Alicia Su, and Linda Olsen Pelky, the

1:38:54 2026 Brooklyn Women of the Year, for

1:38:57 their outstanding and sustained

1:38:59 contributions to the town of Brooklyn

1:39:01 and its residents and extends its

1:39:03 warmest congratulations on the occasion

1:39:06 of their recognition at the Brooklyn

1:39:08 Women of the Year celebration on March

1:39:11 11th, 2026

1:39:14 signed by the Brooklyn Select Board.

1:39:17 Thank you, Michael. Any other

1:39:20 announcements or updates

1:39:25 members that want to go first?

1:39:27 Not me.

1:39:28 Oh,

1:39:29 okay. I have two quick announcements.

1:39:32 Um, first, uh, the budget 101 series

1:39:35 continues next week at the Florida Ruff

1:39:38 and Ridley School at 6:30 p.m. Uh, we

1:39:41 invite all members of the community to

1:39:42 come be a part of that. This will be our

1:39:44 sixth presentation. We've previously had

1:39:46 presentations um at U the BHA

1:39:50 residences, at the Arthur O'Shea

1:39:52 residences at 61 Pine Street, um, at the

1:39:55 Baker School, uh, at Potterham Library,

1:39:57 uh, and online. Um, and it's been a

1:40:01 wonderful experience so far and we've

1:40:03 had really great questions uh and

1:40:05 incisive uh conversations with the

1:40:07 community. So again, next week, next

1:40:09 Wednesday, 6:30 p.m. at Florida Ruff and

1:40:12 Ridley School, the auditorium. Uh, come

1:40:14 one, come all. We look forward to seeing

1:40:16 you there. And on a particularly happy

1:40:18 note, I'm very excited to announce that

1:40:20 the second annual election sticker

1:40:23 contest has opened. The finalists are

1:40:26 online. You can vote for one from each

1:40:29 of the three age groups. Uh they are all

1:40:32 excellent and I wish they could all win.

1:40:35 Um but we will select from those three

1:40:37 finalists. Uh and they each of them will

1:40:40 have their sticker printed and then you

1:40:41 can pick up your Brookline I voted

1:40:44 sticker when you go to vote. Um you can

1:40:46 pick up more than one. Uh you can't vote

1:40:48 more than once. That's illegal. Um but

1:40:51 you can get more than one sticker if you

1:40:53 really really want. Um, so please do

1:40:56 come out and vote. Please do vote for

1:40:58 the stickers, then come out and vote in

1:41:00 the election and get a sticker for

1:41:02 voting in the election. That's it.

1:41:04 Thank you.

1:41:06 Any other

1:41:09 comments or updates? Okay, let's move on

1:41:12 to public comment. Melanie, please

1:41:15 introduce the rules.

1:41:21 Thank you for joining us for public

1:41:22 comment. This is an opportunity for us

1:41:24 to hear your perspective on the issues

1:41:26 in Brookline that matter to you. Each

1:41:28 person speaking tonight is limited to

1:41:30 three minutes. You don't need to use the

1:41:32 entire time, but you may if you like.

1:41:34 Once 15 minutes has been met, there is

1:41:36 an opportunity at the conclusion of the

1:41:38 select board's business for additional

1:41:40 comments. Members of the public

1:41:42 sometimes raise questions during public

1:41:44 comment. We may be able to provide a

1:41:46 quick answer to a question, but are more

1:41:47 likely to work with staff to get a more

1:41:49 thorough answer and respond over email.

1:41:52 The on-screen timer will flash orange

1:41:54 when you have one minute remaining and

1:41:55 red when your time is nearly up. Please

1:41:58 conclude your remarks at that time. If

1:42:00 you have more to say, you're welcome to

1:42:01 send an email to board members

1:42:03 expressing your thoughts in greater

1:42:04 detail. Any person wishing to speak must

1:42:07 begin their comment by identifying

1:42:08 themselves with their full name, either

1:42:10 their Brookline precinct number or

1:42:12 street address, and the specific topic

1:42:14 on which they wish to speak. The board

1:42:16 encourages but does not require that

1:42:18 remote users turn their video on when

1:42:20 commenting.

1:42:22 We did not have anybody sign up for

1:42:24 public comment prior. Is there anybody

1:42:26 in the room that would like to make a

1:42:28 public comment?

1:42:31 Hey, Janet.

1:42:35 Good evening. I'm Janice Khan, town

1:42:38 meeting member from precinct 15, a

1:42:40 member of the town's advisory committee.

1:42:43 I'm also on the comprehensive plan

1:42:45 steering committee and most relevant to

1:42:47 the discussion of the special town

1:42:49 meeting later this evening, a community

1:42:51 member of the Chestnut Hill commercial

1:42:54 area study group. So, in other words, I

1:42:57 take both a neighborhood and a broad

1:42:59 townwide perspective into consideration

1:43:02 when I look at issues. The Chestnut Hill

1:43:05 commercial area study was intended to

1:43:07 look carefully at the commercial area

1:43:09 along Route 9 West in Chestnut Hill and

1:43:12 to create an overlay zoning to

1:43:15 incentivize commercial and mixeduse

1:43:17 development in the entire commercial

1:43:20 area. And I want to spe to emphasize

1:43:22 entire commercial area. To date, that

1:43:25 hasn't happened. Instead, what has

1:43:26 happened is the creation of zoning to

1:43:29 incentivize development for one

1:43:31 developer in one part of the commercial

1:43:33 area. The community advisory group never

1:43:35 heard from any other developer,

1:43:37 including the only other developer in

1:43:39 the Chestnut Hill commercial 4 um zero

1:43:43 district who owns enough property to

1:43:45 create significant development along the

1:43:47 corridor. The fact is that the community

1:43:50 advisory group never heard from any

1:43:52 other property owners and potential

1:43:54 developers in the commercial areas other

1:43:56 three districts. The Chestnut Hill

1:43:58 commercial 3.0, the neighborhood mixed

1:44:01 use on the both on the south side of

1:44:03 Route 9 and the transit oriented

1:44:05 mixeduse on the north side of Route 9

1:44:07 which is in Brooklyn. It abutstar market

1:44:11 and um which is in Newton. So it's right

1:44:13 on the border.

1:44:15 So the reason I wanted to speak tonight

1:44:17 is that um I want to just urge you to

1:44:23 sorry I'm

1:44:23 to defer this

1:44:26 special town meeting

1:44:29 to the fall. So what looks neat on paper

1:44:34 may in fact have realworld

1:44:36 unintended consequences of creating

1:44:39 disincentives for development in a part

1:44:42 of town that could measurably benefit

1:44:45 Brooklyn's commercial tax base.

1:44:48 So I urge you to defer this special town

1:44:50 meeting to a time in the fall. Let's get

1:44:52 the Chestnut Hill commercial area study

1:44:55 community advisory group to finish the

1:44:57 work it was set up to do. and let's get

1:45:00 this zoning right for Brooklyn. Thank

1:45:02 you.

1:45:07 Okay, any other comments from the

1:45:10 audience?

1:49:59 Sure. Um thank you, Mr. Chair. Um the

1:50:02 this project we discovered um

1:50:04 significant termite damage to the

1:50:06 property after the contractors had

1:50:08 removed the building's envelope um to

1:50:11 investigate and insulate the building um

1:50:14 to make it more climate friendly uh

1:50:21 uh that damage and proposing various

1:50:27 advisory board uh in our meeting with

1:50:29 them. Um the this we came up with this

1:50:32 proposal in addition to other work that

1:50:37 advanced to complete the the

1:50:40 preservation of the structure and the um

1:50:43 and the investment of of funds. so that

1:50:46 this will be a an a property that people

1:50:48 can enjoy living in for for hopefully

1:50:56 Okay. So, I'm sorry, you have another

1:50:58 Oh, okay. I sorry. I just want to ask a

1:51:05 is on our miscellaneous agenda. Um, this

1:51:08 is this is a um I'll have to say

1:51:10 extraordinary amount of money when when

1:51:22 from the affordable housing trust. And

1:51:25 you know, I fully trust um the community

1:51:29 preservation act committee to determine

1:51:34 advisory board to determine the use of

1:51:39 the two come together, you know, on one

1:51:47 not on the miscellaneous agenda but on

1:51:57 you know big picture look at if we're

1:52:03 many funds from the community

1:52:04 preservation act with this many funds

1:52:07 from the affordable housing trust u for

1:52:10 one building um you know on boilson

1:52:12 street it's an important building um it

1:52:15 you know it needs to be the problems

1:52:17 with the building need to be addressed

1:52:19 for the sake of the tenants um of that

1:52:22 property

1:52:23 But it we're in the in the range now of

1:52:26 having to question how much do we spend

1:52:29 um per unit to um not add to affordable

1:52:34 housing in Brooklyn but simply maintain

1:52:36 existing affordable housing in Brooklyn.

1:52:39 I don't know what my personal limit is

1:52:42 but because we've never had that

1:52:43 discussion. Um and I think it's a

1:52:46 discussion we're going to have to have

1:52:48 because we're going to be facing

1:52:49 situations like this more than once in

1:52:52 the future. And we can't end up making a

1:52:54 decision one way on, you know, on one uh

1:52:58 request um because we might think that's

1:53:00 an extraordinary amount of money to

1:53:02 spend per unit and then come around and,

1:53:05 you know, make a different decision um

1:53:07 in the opposite direction uh e even

1:53:16 decisions a as to how much being is

1:53:18 being spent per unit because I I don't

1:53:21 know what our limit is on how much we

1:53:23 spend per unit, but I do know that the

1:53:26 more we spend per unit, the fewer the

1:53:29 number of units we are able to provide

1:53:32 as subsidized housing. So, it's an

1:53:34 important question and I'm a little

1:53:36 uncomfortable having to vote on this as

1:53:37 a miscellaneous item tonight.

1:57:53 normally the uh the trust funds as uh as

1:57:59 per per unit cost for u a unit of

1:58:35 spacious family housing, but uh in a

1:58:41 committee um took this on and made a 1.2

2:00:01 members, you know, asked the question,

2:00:31 substantially greater um than the total

2:01:09 to say that um that

2:01:13 there's 23 people living in this

2:01:14 building and maintaining the the best

2:01:17 use of our funds honestly is to make

2:01:19 sure that we don't lose affordable

2:01:21 housing. Um and this is uh this is I

2:01:24 think that situation where um it's not

2:01:26 only losing it, how what would be the

2:01:28 timeline to be able to bundle money in

2:01:32 affordable housing? It would be an

2:01:34 extensive period of time. Um, but I I do

2:01:36 I really do appreciate uh the questions

2:01:39 that you raised, but I'm supportive of

2:01:41 uh of the request to reinforce and

2:01:43 approve what the HAB has already

2:01:44 approved.

2:12:00 Uh my name is Eningga

2:12:02 again.

2:12:03 It's not you, it's me.

2:12:04 Oh,

2:12:06 we've been having a lot of problems

2:12:07 today.

2:12:08 So exciting. Um my name is Eningga

2:12:11 Forment. I moved to Brooklyn

2:12:14 three years and three months ago.

2:12:18 um from North Carolina, from Kentucky,

2:12:21 from California, from Ohio, from

2:12:23 Michigan. So, I'm pretty familiar with

2:12:26 how they do it in many places. Um

2:12:31 I've had a really miscellaneous career.

2:12:33 I started as a librarian for the State

2:12:36 Library of Michigan, which also included

2:12:38 at the time the um National Library

2:12:41 Services for the Blind and Physically

2:12:43 Handicapped. I think at the time it was

2:12:44 just the National Library Services for

2:12:46 the Blind.

2:12:48 Now it's the National Library Services

2:12:49 for the Blind and Print Disabled. So

2:12:52 progress has been made. Um

2:12:55 from there I moved to California as part

2:12:57 of my family life and ended up working

2:13:02 for Huelet Packard as a librarian. Um,

2:13:05 as part of that, I became actually one

2:13:08 of the founding members of the company's

2:13:12 managers of disabled employees group and

2:13:15 helped work on the

2:13:18 post post offer pre-employment physical

2:13:21 process. Um, at the time HP had 140,000

2:13:26 employees, about 40,000 of them in the

2:13:28 California Bay Area and probably another

2:13:31 30,000 in the rest of the country. Um,

2:13:35 from there, thanks to Silicon Valley

2:13:37 Economy, I ended up in Kentucky where I

2:13:41 worked for 10 years for the American

2:13:43 Printing House for the Blind. Um, I did

2:13:46 a number of things there. I was my

2:13:48 primary one. I was part of the 800

2:13:50 number. I did information and referral,

2:13:53 which I was very proud of.

2:13:56 Sorry, I was just talking about the

2:13:57 other day to someone and one of the

2:14:00 things, one of the questions I got often

2:14:02 than I would like is I would get calls

2:14:04 from parents of children who had brain

2:14:06 tumors. And many times brain tumors in

2:14:10 children affect the optic nerves or the

2:14:12 function of the optical centers of the

2:14:14 brain. And it would be my

2:14:17 excuse me responsibility to welcome

2:14:20 welcome them if you can call it

2:14:22 welcoming to yet another system the

2:14:25 blindness system and hopefully get some

2:14:28 useful information to them some

2:14:31 educational materials for as long as

2:14:33 their child was able to participate in

2:14:34 the school system and so forth. Um

2:14:40 I also worked with what you might call

2:14:43 celebrities. They're I'm sure you know

2:14:45 who they are. Um, blind performance

2:14:48 celebrities out there and they would

2:14:50 call us for materials because we were

2:14:52 the central we're in and are the central

2:14:53 clearing house for braille and large

2:14:57 print materials in the United States. We

2:14:59 have a database had a database called

2:15:00 the Louisie database which is still

2:15:02 there which is free for the public to

2:15:04 use to find materials that have been

2:15:08 produced in various accessible formats.

2:15:11 Um, now there's an electronic format as

2:15:13 well, which is excellent. Um, let's see.

2:15:18 Then

2:15:20 after I retired, we ended up in North

2:15:22 Carolina. Uh, Louisville, Kentucky is

2:15:25 kind of a squirrely place to live. Let's

2:15:26 just say quality of life was declining

2:15:28 as they kept paving more and more of the

2:15:30 city to less and less good effect. And

2:15:33 um, there I was a pole worker for eight

2:15:39 years. I came here. I'm a pole worker

2:15:41 again. I'm also a member of the as John

2:15:45 Vincecoyak knows

2:15:48 the age friendly cities group. And um

2:15:53 I'm relatively late in life to

2:15:55 disability myself. My husband of almost

2:15:58 30 years was blind from birth and I was

2:16:01 active in blindness

2:16:05 issues

2:16:06 during his lifetime of various sorts. um

2:16:10 and

2:16:12 sort of overlapping with age friendly

2:16:14 cities, a number of the issues that were

2:16:16 coming up. Well, yes, there's a

2:16:18 designation this is an age friendly

2:16:20 business, but I use

2:16:23 many of those businesses. Some of them

2:16:25 are old favorites of mine, but they may

2:16:29 be age friendly, but they're not

2:16:30 necessarily disability friendly.

2:16:33 And

2:16:35 knowing that Brooklyn was a town that

2:16:37 did things unlike other places I lived,

2:16:40 I just wanted to be part of that. And I

2:16:43 think it was the MBTA actually coughing

2:16:46 up money to make the the tea stations

2:16:48 accessible that got me really interested

2:16:50 because before it was like, yeah, if I'm

2:16:52 going somewhere, I look on the map, I

2:16:55 say, what's the closest tea station? My

2:16:57 next question is, is that tea station

2:16:58 accessible? Which many of them weren't?

2:17:02 And as a result, I've never been to

2:17:03 Cleveland Circle or any of the

2:17:04 businesses around there because it's

2:17:06 such a pain in the butt on the tea and

2:17:07 there's no bus.

2:17:09 Okay,

2:17:12 let us ask you a few questions.

2:17:13 Sure.

2:17:14 To get a sense of, you know, what you

2:17:15 would like to bring to the Disabilities

2:17:17 Commission. Um, I see you've been in a

2:17:20 lot of states in the country. I mean, is

2:17:22 there anything that you've learned from

2:17:23 what they do or what what people do in

2:17:26 in other states that would be helpful

2:17:28 here in Brooklyn? ideas that you can

2:17:31 sort of steal from them and and and give

2:17:33 to us.

2:17:34 Well, one thing I've learned, it's a

2:17:36 negative thing, and I'm sure you're

2:17:37 familiar with the phenomenon, is nothing

2:17:39 is done until something awful happens.

2:17:43 And um I would like not to see anything

2:17:46 awful happen. And one thing that a new

2:17:48 story you may, how could you not

2:17:50 remember? A few months ago, a a w an

2:17:53 elderly woman was thrown off a bus

2:17:56 basically in Boston because she happened

2:17:58 to have a a a shopping cart, but she

2:18:03 could have just as been well have been

2:18:04 me with my walker because there is an

2:18:07 issue on the tea. The tea has been

2:18:09 working its butt off and doing a pretty

2:18:11 good job of becoming accessible.

2:18:13 But everybody has been ignoring the real

2:18:16 elephant in the room, which is shopping

2:18:18 carts, which I brought up at the meeting

2:18:20 today, John. And nobody really realizes

2:18:24 what a freaking traffic jam you can have

2:18:27 on the 66 bus when you have four people

2:18:30 uh with big shopping carts and one or

2:18:33 two people with walkers or even a

2:18:34 wheelchair just trying to get off or on

2:18:37 the bus.

2:18:39 And when she was thrown when the woman

2:18:42 in Boston was thrown off the bus, I was

2:18:44 like, "Yeah,

2:18:46 I've seen crowds be very accepting and

2:18:50 pleasant. I've also seen crowds be

2:18:52 somewhat hostile. And I've seen crowds

2:18:54 be pretty hostile to someone who's

2:18:56 blocking the way. And

2:19:00 so that's an example of how not to deal

2:19:03 with things essentially. But Brooklyn

2:19:07 offers forums the likes of which I have

2:19:10 never seen any place I've ever lived.

2:19:11 And part of it is because of the way

2:19:13 Massachusetts does its governance.

2:19:16 If this were in

2:19:18 Orange County, North Carolina,

2:19:21 you would have Orange County,

2:19:24 you would have Chapel Hill or you would

2:19:26 have the Chapel Hill and Carro School

2:19:28 District,

2:19:30 etc. So, there are all sorts of layers

2:19:32 of governance here. It's all in one

2:19:34 spot, which just blew the socks off me

2:19:37 when I first moved here. I go, "What

2:19:39 about the county?"

2:19:40 Okay.

2:19:41 I don't want to talk about the county.

2:19:43 Yeah. It's like, yeah.

2:19:44 Okay. And in Kentucky, it was the same

2:19:47 way, only different. So that I think is

2:19:48 is one of the strengths Brookline has is

2:19:51 it's one-stop shopping essentially. And

2:19:54 the but not only that, but the

2:19:56 departments talk to each other.

2:19:58 Could can let's ask or allow other

2:20:03 Sure.

2:20:04 Okay. Michael,

2:20:06 um thank you very much and thank you for

2:20:08 your application. Um I'm I'm

2:20:11 particularly intrigued by your uh the

2:20:14 work that you've done on the age

2:20:15 friendly cities and and the the

2:20:19 commonalities that you have identified

2:20:21 between the needs of that community and

2:20:23 the needs of the disab disability

2:20:25 community. Um, so I'm wondering, you

2:20:28 know, in this era of um always looking

2:20:31 for uh more efficient forms of

2:20:34 government and uh things that we can do

2:20:36 that help multiple constituencies.

2:20:39 Whether um you see your role um on those

2:20:43 two bodies being in any way

2:20:45 complimentary or uh you that there are

2:20:48 opportunities to bring those two groups

2:20:50 together for more effective advocacy or

2:20:54 or outcomes.

2:20:56 I was

2:20:58 a little surprised at the beginning

2:21:00 because there wasn't much overlapping

2:21:02 because of the issues that were on the

2:21:04 table. But as time has gone on,

2:21:07 what that group does is they bring in

2:21:09 other departments to talk about things

2:21:11 like sidewalk repairs. And was it last

2:21:13 month or the month before they were

2:21:15 talking about there's a new technology

2:21:17 for dealing with the irregular the the

2:21:19 sidewalks that aren't level where they

2:21:21 basically saw one off and give a rather

2:21:23 comfortable

2:21:25 mound as opposed to before where they

2:21:27 just kept adding asphalt and adding

2:21:29 asphalt and you had this little mountain

2:21:31 you had to navigate over. And that was

2:21:33 you know that was very relevant to all

2:21:35 of us. And when they talk about um parks

2:21:37 and recreation comes in and gives a talk

2:21:39 about an event, they will address issues

2:21:42 like is there going to be a lot of

2:21:44 handicap parking? Is there going to be a

2:21:45 place for the the Ubers and the lifts to

2:21:48 drop people off and things like that. So

2:21:51 they are already bringing people in in

2:21:54 response to issues.

2:21:56 Thank you, David. I noticed on your

2:22:00 resume that you also worked in business

2:22:02 security systems and that makes me

2:22:05 curious. Do you look at the purview of

2:22:08 being on the disability commission as

2:22:11 more of a physical accessibility issue

2:22:15 or do you see do you have a more

2:22:17 expanded vision about anything really

2:22:20 that can impact that population whether

2:22:23 it's physical or something from the

2:22:25 virtual world?

2:22:26 Well, as as a person who's using this

2:22:29 and will probably eventually end up in a

2:22:30 wheelchair. The physical access is super

2:22:33 important. I mean that's pure quality of

2:22:35 life. However, as a librarian who was

2:22:37 also an information and referral person

2:22:40 and who also did databases, information

2:22:42 is vital, especially in today's world.

2:22:45 There's accessible technology that

2:22:47 people could be unaware of. There are

2:22:50 systems relating to certain things like

2:22:53 rehabilitation

2:22:55 um and whatnot that many people don't

2:22:57 know about. Um there's funding. For

2:23:00 instance, uh the laws are different now

2:23:03 and I haven't dealt with it, but in my

2:23:06 daughter's time, there was um a disabled

2:23:10 child, depending on the disability, of

2:23:12 course, could receive SSI and

2:23:15 potentially Medicare, depending on

2:23:17 family funding,

2:23:19 from birth,

2:23:21 which a lot of people don't know when

2:23:23 they struggle when they have a special

2:23:24 needs child to get what they need to

2:23:29 give the

2:23:30 a chance to grow and develop and have a

2:23:32 normal life.

2:23:34 And um

2:23:37 I don't know, having not directly had

2:23:39 someone involved, how the school system

2:23:42 and the social workers handle that in

2:23:44 Brookline. It sounds like they're doing

2:23:46 a pretty good job, but there's always

2:23:47 room for improvement there because

2:23:50 systems overlap, technologies overlap.

2:23:54 Um they're all distinct systems, but

2:23:56 you've got to know how to to work them

2:23:58 in order to maximize your benefits.

2:24:02 Thank you. Uh any other questions?

2:24:05 John?

2:24:05 Well, I just want to make uh the comment

2:24:07 as someone who has had the pleasure um

2:24:26 three three years, three books.

2:24:49 you know, so I'll stop there, but um uh

2:25:06 that's a great thing.

2:25:07 Oh, Brookline feels like home.

2:32:29 Um, I have a question uh about something

2:32:33 you said earlier. Um, that this was

2:32:35 something that we do annually. Um, and I

2:32:37 realize that these are 10-year bonds, so

2:32:40 with equal amounts over 10 years. Have

2:32:42 we been doing this for more than 10

2:32:44 years? So, are there other bonds that

2:32:46 are rolling off as these are rolling on?

2:32:51 Yes, they they do come off, you know,

2:32:53 after we pay them in full. And this

2:32:56 program, at least on the sewer side, is

2:32:59 part of a 16-year program, and we

2:33:01 anticipate wrapping it up in 2032. So,

2:33:06 you know, roughly halfway through the

2:33:07 program. So, some of the money that we

2:33:09 have been borrowing is coming off, but

2:33:11 yeah, we continue to to acrue some debt,

2:33:14 but it's so worth it in the long run.

2:33:17 You know, we're going to get a tight

2:33:18 system. Not only is it a tight system,

2:33:20 but it's also protecting the asset. Um,

2:33:23 lining and sealing the manholes just

2:33:25 extends the the life of the asset um

2:33:29 much longer than you would by not doing

2:33:31 any preventative maintenance or rehab

2:33:33 work.

2:41:27 into the um status slide. Um so um last

2:41:32 year we uh welcomed three new members uh

2:41:35 Bill Heinman, Patricia Korea and uh Alan

2:41:38 Levton uh who brought you know valuable

2:41:40 focus uh in transportation, municipal

2:41:42 law and regulatory matters and

2:41:44 engineering respectively.

2:41:47 Yes, I'll try. Um the sustainability

2:41:50 division um also welcomes some new

2:41:52 members. uh Shelley Dean who's just

2:41:54 started in June of 2025 as deputy

2:41:57 director, program manager Katie Weather

2:42:00 seat seat who started in September 2025

2:42:03 and we also um welcomed I think Caroline

2:42:06 Stout is that right? um in December as a

2:42:10 community first partnership. Um so it

2:42:13 was a year of growth for both the

2:42:15 sustainability division and also for ZAB

2:42:17 and it's really important for us because

2:42:19 um I think we had struggled in past

2:42:21 years to make progress without staff

2:42:23 support. It's been very helpful to have

2:42:25 additional members on the board.

2:42:29 Next slide.

2:42:32 our major initiatives for last year. Um

2:42:35 uh we went through a large process for

2:42:38 the climate action and resiliency plan.

2:42:41 Um the resiliency portion of this is not

2:42:44 really a ZAB focus but we participated

2:42:46 in the whole process. Uh there was

2:42:48 substantial outreach to the community um

2:42:53 which was le led by the sustainability

2:42:55 division. Zab's role was largely a

2:42:58 advisory role. We helped develop a uh

2:43:01 prioritization framework um which has

2:43:03 been used to prioritize the

2:43:07 goals, strategies and actions in the KRP

2:43:09 which is now in draft format and um

2:43:12 which has been transitioned to the

2:43:14 sustainability division for their

2:43:16 ownership so that they can take it from

2:43:17 here and use that framework to uh

2:43:21 prioritize both current init current

2:43:24 efforts but also future efforts as the

2:43:26 the uh sort of zero emission

2:43:29 uh

2:43:34 arena changes which is changing quite

2:43:36 quickly. Um the other big effort for

2:43:40 this last year was um we presented last

2:43:42 year that we were going to focus on two

2:43:43 things as initiatives. One which one of

2:43:45 which was municipal solar and the second

2:43:48 thing was um Berto the municipal solar

2:43:51 initiative um was an interesting and as

2:43:56 a roller coaster for last year. Um, it

2:43:58 started as a a uh ZAB initiative where

2:44:01 we looked at and analyzed municipal

2:44:03 solar and how it could be an effective

2:44:06 uh tool for both reducing our greenhouse

2:44:09 gas emissions but also a uh a tool for

2:44:14 um making the town money through the

2:44:17 year with a lot of advocacy from

2:44:19 specifically Sergio Modilani. Um and

2:44:22 then at the end of the year as part of

2:44:24 the futures subcommittee to the

2:44:26 expenditures and revenue committee um we

2:44:30 managed to move the solar initiative

2:44:33 from a very theoretical thing to a

2:44:36 um a plan which the sustainability

2:44:38 division um and DTPW put together which

2:44:42 has two tracks a near-term ITC capture

2:44:45 track. So the ITC is going away um very

2:44:48 soon u by December 2027 and we will be

2:44:52 able to ideally capture some portion of

2:44:54 the ITC that will help pay for the solar

2:44:56 that we'll put in our roofs which will

2:44:58 in turn help pay for electricity in the

2:45:01 town of Brooklyn. Um it will also

2:45:04 obviously help us with our zero

2:45:06 emissions goals. There's a second track

2:45:08 which is a longerterm expansion.

2:45:09 Excuse me. Um I don't like uh acronyms.

2:45:14 ITC means investment tax credit.

2:45:16 Investment tax credit. Yes, that's

2:45:18 right.

2:45:18 I was gonna say

2:45:21 um and if I'm speaking too quickly, let

2:45:24 me know. I just don't want to take up

2:45:25 more than five minutes. Um there's a

2:45:27 long-term expansion. So, as part of this

2:45:29 effort, this plan that Tampa put

2:45:31 together, uh we're looking at planning

2:45:33 and sequencing for 17 additional sites.

2:45:36 Um and that's something that will be

2:45:38 happening next or starting now but going

2:45:41 into next year. Um I think

2:45:47 we had some working groups established.

2:45:48 So we were planned to establish a

2:45:50 residential working group and a

2:45:52 commercial working group. We

2:45:53 successfully established the uh

2:45:55 residential working group although we

2:45:56 had to wait for the the community first

2:45:58 energy advocate to join. Um so we really

2:46:01 had the first meeting of that in January

2:46:04 of this year. We established a Berdo

2:46:07 working group which is a precursor to

2:46:08 the commercial working group for deep

2:46:10 analysis of Berdo and to look into that

2:46:12 to determine whether or not that was

2:46:14 something that the town should do.

2:46:18 Uh next slide policy achievements and

2:46:20 key votes. Um some policy achievements

2:46:23 and to be clear these are mainly driven

2:46:25 by the uh the sustainability division.

2:46:28 We have a municipal decarbonization

2:46:30 roadmap adopted. Zero emissions vehicle

2:46:32 policy adopted. Washington Street

2:46:34 complete streets supported and climate

2:46:36 leaders application submitted.

2:46:39 I don't know if we've heard yet. We have

2:46:41 not. Um we had 10 key votes. 10

2:46:44 substantive votes were taken, six

2:46:46 directed towards the select board.

2:46:50 Next slide. Challenges and lessons

2:46:52 learned. Um we one of the votes we

2:46:56 directed towards the site board was not

2:46:58 taken up by the slight board. Um

2:46:59 principally because it was not a

2:47:01 townspecific policy. It was more of a

2:47:03 state policy. Um we should probably not

2:47:07 have made that vote. Um we have since

2:47:09 then I have since then established uh

2:47:12 regular communication with Tommy Bull to

2:47:14 just kind of check in on state

2:47:16 initiatives and policies to make sure

2:47:17 that we're we have some coordination.

2:47:20 Um the um

2:47:23 mass safe programs face legislative

2:47:25 challenges and we and we uh we had a

2:47:28 vote go forward on that and we had a

2:47:31 challenge with the their December GEP

2:47:33 vote which required a special board

2:47:35 meeting of the slight board on December

2:47:37 23rd which we thank you for. Apologies

2:47:39 that we did not have better visibility

2:47:42 into that. Um the other challenge was

2:47:45 Berto complexity. Um just there was some

2:47:49 concern from the community. There was a

2:47:51 lot of complexity around Berto and it

2:47:54 was a great learning but it was a

2:47:55 challenge.

2:47:56 Nick, can you take a couple seconds to

2:47:58 explain what Berto is?

2:48:00 Yes. So

2:48:00 thank you.

2:48:01 Berto is the building emission reduction

2:48:05 ordinance

2:48:06 um that is has been implemented by

2:48:09 principally Boston Cambridge.

2:48:12 um also additionally by Newton in a

2:48:14 lesser form and some other

2:48:15 municipalities.

2:48:17 The goal of Berdo is to um have a

2:48:24 alternative compliance payment for uh

2:48:27 buildings that do not meet environmental

2:48:30 standards. And the there are sort of

2:48:33 three categories of buildings that are

2:48:36 generally looked at. one of which is

2:48:37 commercial buildings, one of which is

2:48:39 large uh residential buildings and then

2:48:42 kind of everything else. And each town

2:48:45 has taken a different approach. Each

2:48:46 town and city has taken a different

2:48:48 approach to those. And so what we did in

2:48:49 the burden analysis is we looked at what

2:48:51 was appropriate for a town like

2:48:53 Brooklyn, what might be politically

2:48:56 acceptable, what the impact would be

2:48:58 from an environmental perspective, what

2:48:59 the costs might be. And we use that

2:49:02 initiative as a

2:49:04 template for the prioritization

2:49:06 framework to better understand how that

2:49:07 could be applied to other initiatives.

2:49:11 Is that helpful? Okay. Um and then the

2:49:15 final challenge is a very obvious one

2:49:16 which is the uh should say

2:49:21 the investment tax credits are expiring.

2:49:24 uh we are trying to take what advantage

2:49:25 we can and again we thank the

2:49:27 sustainability division and the DPW for

2:49:29 working with us on the municipal solar

2:49:31 initiative.

2:49:33 Looking ahead to 2026 um we are looking

2:49:36 at the carp finalization and adoption.

2:49:38 We had a

2:49:40 um a draft sent out recently and it's in

2:49:45 good shape I think. Um once that is out

2:49:48 we'll obviously be working on those carp

2:49:50 goals, strategies and actions. I would

2:49:53 note it's a 10-year plan. We have three

2:49:55 different uh tiers of goal, strategy,

2:49:59 and actions. Uh the intention is over

2:50:01 the next 10 years that we should be able

2:50:03 to hit all three. Uh so all of the items

2:50:08 in there, but we're trying to prioritize

2:50:10 those that we can hit first because

2:50:13 they're more important based upon the

2:50:14 framework. But we also recognize that we

2:50:16 might not be able to get to some of

2:50:17 them. So we may in fact start picking

2:50:19 things from tier two or tier three as

2:50:21 appropriate.

2:50:22 Um the other things we'll be focusing on

2:50:24 are the municipal solar implementation

2:50:26 although that's mainly in the hands of

2:50:28 the sustainability division and DPW and

2:50:32 probably more looking at the track to

2:50:33 municipal solar planning and community

2:50:35 engagement.

2:50:37 Um we'll also be continuing to work on

2:50:39 the residents working group and try and

2:50:41 evolve the commercial working group into

2:50:44 um try and start the commercial working

2:50:45 group uh evolving from the Berto working

2:50:48 group

2:50:49 and then we can go to discussions and

2:50:51 questions. Before any discussion or

2:50:54 questions I do have a very long

2:50:56 narrative which I've put together which

2:50:58 I didn't send out with the packet but I

2:51:00 will be sending out if anyone wants to

2:51:01 read it. So if any of your questions are

2:51:03 not answered they will probably be

2:51:05 answered in that packet.

2:51:07 So, thank you,

2:51:09 David.

2:51:10 So, you talked about uh that one of the

2:51:12 goals was to take full advantage of the

2:51:14 inflation reduction act incentives. Now,

2:51:17 my understanding is that originally uh

2:51:20 so that included a 30% residential tax

2:51:23 credit on solar. It was initially set to

2:51:26 expire in 2032.

2:51:28 when the uh big beautiful bill as it's

2:51:31 called passed that moved the expiration

2:51:34 date to December 31st 2025. So we're

2:51:38 already past that expiration date. So

2:51:40 were there additional grants that were

2:51:43 sought

2:51:44 that was for residential. So we're

2:51:45 looking at I think for the town we have

2:51:47 two dates. We have um any uh project

2:51:52 placed in service by July 3rd or or

2:51:56 something of this year would be

2:51:58 eligible. Okay.

2:51:59 I don't think we're going to hit that

2:52:00 date.

2:52:01 Um being placed not placed in service uh

2:52:03 where the project has started. So that

2:52:05 would include basically making a

2:52:06 commitment to someone who is going to do

2:52:09 the project, a financial commitment or

2:52:12 projects which completed by December

2:52:14 31st, 2027 will be eligible for between

2:52:17 30 and 40% depending upon the source of

2:52:19 the solar panels

2:52:21 and that's specific to commercial.

2:52:24 Um it's it the town can take advantage

2:52:27 of it.

2:52:27 The town

2:52:28 is the important part.

2:52:29 All right.

2:52:31 And that is part of the plan that the

2:52:33 sustainability division put together um

2:52:36 for

2:52:39 this year that this the phase one and

2:52:40 phase two. This is they're phase one is

2:52:42 trying to take advantage of those

2:52:43 credits and save the town some money,

2:52:45 get some federal funding.

2:52:46 Okay.

2:52:52 Can I just

2:52:52 Sure.

2:52:53 Just uh Nick, first thank you so much.

2:52:55 Um is this your first or second time in

2:52:57 front of us? Second time.

2:52:58 It's my second time. It's

2:52:58 your second time. Well done. Very

2:53:00 efficient. Um, so just to be clear, we

2:53:03 started Berto investigated and then

2:53:05 we're we're not going to pursue it. Is

2:53:07 that my read of this?

2:53:09 Um, I think it's unfortunately.

2:53:11 Yes.

2:53:12 A bit a bit more complicated than that.

2:53:14 Uh, Berto turned out to be uh priority

2:53:16 two. So it's not in the top tier of

2:53:19 things that we would focus on.

2:53:20 Okay.

2:53:20 This is a 10-year plan where we hope to

2:53:22 hit everything in the carp, which would

2:53:25 include Berto.

2:53:26 Yeah. Um when the city of Boston started

2:53:29 their Berto initiative, it took years

2:53:31 for them to get it moving forward. So

2:53:33 it's not an overnight thing. It's not

2:53:35 like something we we switch on.

2:53:37 Um if we look at out 10 years and we try

2:53:40 and you know kind of work back from that

2:53:41 10-year period and we say we want to

2:53:43 have something like Berto in place,

2:53:45 it is likely that we need to start

2:53:46 working on the next couple of years in

2:53:49 some form.

2:53:49 And to be clear what Berto I know you

2:53:51 explained it, but in simple terms, Berto

2:53:54 places a requirement on large buildings

2:53:57 either large commercial

2:53:58 Yep. or

2:53:59 or large and or large residential or

2:54:01 everyone

2:54:01 and they have and they have to measure

2:54:03 their energy consumption and at some

2:54:04 point they start getting dinged for not

2:54:07 reducing their energy consumption.

2:54:08 Correct. Essentially

2:54:09 that that is a flavor of it. Yes. So um

2:54:12 I think what ZAB determined was that um

2:54:15 we would probably recommend we would

2:54:18 likely recommend something which focused

2:54:19 on commercial buildings first because

2:54:21 that's what everyone else has done. And

2:54:23 we also were looking at uh ways to

2:54:29 trying to look at ways to incentivize

2:54:31 people to do things in other ways.

2:54:32 We don't need to dig into I just want to

2:54:33 make sure that it's kind of it's a tier

2:54:35 two. So it's

2:54:35 it is a tier two.

2:54:58 Next, uh we have grant of location, a a

2:55:01 grant of location for the MBTA.

2:55:04 Um

2:55:06 and I have to read some stuff.

2:55:10 The next items on tonight's agenda

2:55:12 relate to the MBTA Green Line C branch

2:55:15 accessibility project along Beacon

2:55:18 Street. The MBTA is working to upgrade

2:55:22 several stations in Brooklyn to meet ADA

2:55:24 accessibility requirements. Mhm.

2:55:26 The select board approved amended grants

2:55:29 of location for two stations earlier

2:55:31 this fall and tonight we are considering

2:55:34 the remaining five stations that require

2:55:36 amended grants of location in order to

2:55:39 construct the accessible platforms and

2:55:42 related improvements.

2:55:45 The board received a full project

2:55:48 presentation from the MBTA on February

2:55:50 24th and the transportation board

2:55:53 reviewed the plans on February 25th and

2:55:57 voted in support of the parking and lane

2:55:59 reduction required for the project.

2:56:03 This evening, the select board will

2:56:05 consider three related items.

2:56:10 We will have a public hearing and

2:56:13 possible vote on the amended grants of

2:56:15 location.

2:56:17 Uh we will also address supporting

2:56:19 transportation board direction to staff

2:56:22 regarding the U-turn movement near Hall

2:56:25 Street

2:56:27 and we will discuss a memorandum of

2:56:29 understanding between the MBTA and the

2:56:32 town.

2:56:36 So let's start off with the first item

2:56:40 uh which is the public hearing on the

2:56:41 MBTA's request for amended grants

2:56:44 location for

2:56:46 um following station Hall Street St.

2:56:49 Paul Street Summit Avenue

2:56:52 Brandon Hall Fairbanks and Dean Road.

2:56:56 And we'll start with a presentation from

2:56:58 the MBTA describing the requested grants

2:57:01 of location.

2:57:06 Who wrote this?

2:57:26 Good evening. Like to thank the select

2:57:45 Tonight we're seeking an amendment to

2:57:47 the grant of location. As was discussed,

2:57:49 this is a grant of location that dates

2:57:51 back to 1897

2:57:53 and allows for the C branch to operate

2:57:55 today and will allow us to modernize the

2:57:57 C branch going forward. We'll cover the

2:57:59 stations that are being improved, the

2:58:01 timeline, and provide a recap of our

2:58:04 recent work with Brooklyn's various

2:58:05 boards and committees.

2:58:09 yourself for the record?

2:58:16 and do work for the MBTA.

2:58:22 Uh so, as I was saying, tonight we will

2:58:24 uh seek that amendment to the grant of

2:58:26 location. We'll cover the stations that

2:58:27 are being improved, the timeline, and

2:58:29 provide a recap of our recent work with

2:58:31 Brookline's various boards and

2:58:32 committees. We'll conclude by detailing

2:58:34 a specific a specific amendment to that

2:58:36 grant of location.

2:58:39 So, currently there are nine

2:58:41 inaccessible stations on the C branch.

2:58:43 This project will upgrade all of those

2:58:45 stations by widening and raising the

2:58:47 platforms to accommodate mobility

2:58:49 devices by removing platform

2:58:50 obstructions, installing platform

2:58:52 lighting and signage, and upgrade

2:58:54 pedestrian crossings to the platforms.

2:58:56 As part of this project, we will combine

2:58:58 Brandon Hall and Fairbank Street into a

2:59:00 single station that will accommodate the

2:59:01 new type 10 trains and can serve will

2:59:03 allow them to serve all the stations

2:59:05 along the line. We will also close Kent

2:59:07 Street station due to its proximity to

2:59:09 Haw Street.

2:59:20 Pardon me. So those uh issues that I

2:59:22 described on the stations are shown here

2:59:24 on the screen. You can see that there's

2:59:25 limited protection from traffic.

2:59:27 Stations are overcrowded, platforms are

2:59:29 narrow, uh we have limited way finding

2:59:31 and steep slopes. There are also various

2:59:34 issues with obstacles in the way such as

2:59:36 poles and trees and then a lack of

2:59:38 lighting. And as I mentioned, we would

2:59:39 address those.

3:01:10 track.

3:01:12 So, this project is the result of

3:01:13 extensive collaboration between the MBTA

3:01:16 and the town of Brooklyn. Through

3:01:17 numerous engagements with residents,

3:01:18 elected officials, and stakeholders,

3:01:20 we've developed a project that will

3:01:22 deliver significant improvements and

3:01:23 minimize impacts. Over the last few

3:01:26 weeks, we've secured feedback and

3:01:28 support from the tree planting

3:01:29 committee, the commission on

3:01:30 accessibility, the transportation board,

3:01:32 the select board, and the public as we

3:01:34 finalized the project design plans. We

3:01:37 will return to Brookline in April for a

3:01:39 pre-construction meeting at which time

3:01:41 we will describe the actual construction

3:01:42 details and plans. Construction will

3:01:45 start shortly after the marathon in late

3:01:47 April.

3:01:52 With the received approval from the tree

3:01:53 planting committee and the select board,

3:01:56 this project will remove 50 young trees

3:01:58 and 46 mature trees. This includes the

3:02:00 construction of two ramps at Brandon

3:02:02 Hall which is required for equal access

3:02:05 and compliance with accessibility laws.

3:02:08 The MBTA has set aside dedicated funding

3:02:10 which with which will use to replant 98

3:02:13 trees along the Beacon Street corridor.

3:02:15 Any remaining funds that are available

3:02:17 will be used for additional trees at the

3:02:19 town's discretion.

3:02:23 With the received approval from the

3:02:25 transportation board, the project will

3:02:26 update five crosswalks, 11 track

3:02:28 crossings. We will retain all existing

3:02:31 accessible parking spaces, but we will

3:02:33 remove 22 parking spaces, two angled

3:02:35 spaces, and 20 parallel spaces. And

3:02:38 along Beacon Street eastbound, one lane

3:02:40 will be removed at Haw Street to

3:02:42 accommodate the new station and the

3:02:43 accessible curb. At the direction of the

3:02:46 transportation board, Brooklyn Town

3:02:48 staff will conduct a traffic analysis to

3:02:50 investigate the best use of the

3:02:51 remaining lanes and how to provide

3:02:53 access in that area. So, with that

3:02:55 summary, I will provide

3:02:58 the technology to Gwen, who is obviously

3:03:00 much better at it than

3:03:04 Thank you, Matt. Uh, my name is Gwen

3:03:06 Dunvy. I'm the deputy chief of

3:03:08 integrated transit programs. I oversee

3:03:10 the green line transformation group

3:03:11 which uh manages a holistic group of

3:03:13 projects modernizing the green line. I'm

3:10:48 seven of those stations.

3:10:51 And with that, like to open it up to any

3:10:53 questions anyone may have.

3:10:55 Okay, any questions from the board?

3:11:01 I knew you'd have a question.

3:11:02 I actually don't have a question. I have

3:11:03 a compliment.

3:11:04 Oh, okay.

3:11:05 Would that be okay?

3:11:05 Yes, that's fine.

3:11:06 Am I authorized to give a compliment?

3:11:08 Always authorized.

3:11:09 Um, so Michael and I have had uh the

3:11:12 pleasure. It's usually been a pleasure

3:11:14 to work with the MBTA and staff and, you

3:11:18 know, committees and Brian. Um, and I

3:11:22 was skeptical going into it. I admit

3:11:24 working with the MBTA when I was asked

3:11:26 to participate like what am I getting

3:11:27 myself into? Um but I will say it's been

3:11:30 a pleasure. Um the willingness of the

3:11:32 MBTA to partner. Um I know it it's it's

3:11:36 probably it wasn't seen publicly because

3:11:38 the public wasn't behind the scenes kind

3:11:40 of seeing these things. But you did make

3:11:43 a lot of concessions and you were

3:11:45 cooperative. Um you sometimes

3:11:47 begrudgingly made changes, but you made

3:11:49 the changes. Um I think to the benefit

3:11:51 of our community, certainly for the

3:11:53 benefit of our community, we had a

3:11:54 interview tonight for the disabilities

3:11:56 commission who raised issues of

3:11:58 accessibility. Um and uh it's so

3:12:02 important for our community to have

3:12:04 accessible transportation and access to

3:12:06 the tea. But this is coming at a high

3:12:08 cost. Um you know, we don't discount the

3:12:11 loss of mature trees, uh the tree

3:12:14 canopy. um we are making significant

3:12:16 sacrifices uh to partner with you to

3:12:19 grant this location. Um but I personally

3:12:22 think it's it's worth it. Um I look

3:12:24 forward to the trees growing back and

3:12:26 getting bigger and hopefully we'll leave

3:12:27 enough room for them to grow and not

3:12:29 have to uh impede the tracks like they

3:12:32 have in the past. But I just want to say

3:12:33 thank you to everybody who's been

3:12:35 involved. Um it was a tremendous amount

3:12:39 of work to have this uh have this get to

3:12:42 this point. Um, I just want to assure

3:12:44 you, um, and it's I' I've really enjoyed

3:12:46 being part of it. So, thank you so much.

3:12:50 Okay. Any other questions?

3:12:52 We're we're just discussing 11A at this

3:12:55 point.

3:12:55 Yep.

3:12:55 Yeah. Okay.

3:12:59 Okay. If there are no other questions

3:13:00 from the select board, let's open up the

3:13:02 meeting or the uh public comment period.

3:13:05 So, we have Alyssa P has signed up. I

3:13:08 don't see her online.

3:13:10 She's right.

3:13:12 You have two hands online.

3:13:14 Um, Alisa Plazanja, precinct 8. Uh, two

3:13:17 weeks ago, you received a petition from

3:13:20 176 Brookline residents asking you to

3:13:23 take action on three items related to

3:13:25 this project um for the seaine. You

3:13:29 declined to act on Brandon Hall

3:13:30 Fairbanks, but you didn't respond to the

3:13:33 other two issues raised in the petition.

3:13:36 So, I'm here tonight to ask you to

3:13:38 respond to them, please. The first was

3:13:41 um will you commit to more comprehensive

3:13:45 ecological restoration? So this is above

3:13:47 and beyond the replanting that the MBTA

3:13:50 will be um providing. So there are spots

3:13:53 along the way that are currently barren.

3:13:54 So to the extent that the tea is really

3:13:56 just replacing what might be lost, would

3:13:59 you will the town of Brookline look to

3:14:02 expand the plantings beyond what the

3:14:05 MBTA is doing? The second question is,

3:14:08 will you ask the MBTA for their

3:14:09 commitment that they will not harm any

3:14:12 wildlife during this work? This question

3:14:14 may be an issue of just clarifying

3:14:16 terminology as the MBTA has stated on

3:14:19 the record that they will comply with

3:14:21 all state and federal environmental

3:14:23 regulations, but it's my understanding

3:14:26 that state and federal law does not

3:14:28 protect common species such as squirrels

3:14:31 and non-migratory birds such as house

3:14:34 sparrows and starings. Since there are

3:14:36 55 mature trees slated for removal,

3:14:39 that's including the other two stations

3:14:41 that you've already approved. It

3:14:43 certainly seems quite possible, given

3:14:46 that we're entering nesting season and

3:14:47 that the work here isn't going to begin

3:14:49 until April that some of these trees may

3:14:52 contain nests with baby sparrows,

3:14:54 starings, or baby squirrels. And as a

3:14:57 result, merely complying with the law

3:14:59 could lead to baby animals being killed

3:15:00 if the tree in which their nest is

3:15:02 located is removed. Now, I would hope

3:15:05 that neither the select board nor the

3:15:07 MBTA would want any innocent baby

3:15:09 animals to be harmed as a result of this

3:15:11 project. So, will you ask the MBTA for

3:15:14 their explicit commitment that no

3:15:16 nesting animals of any kind will be

3:15:18 displaced or harmed as a result of this

3:15:20 work as we asked you in the petition?

3:15:22 Thank you.

3:15:30 There's nothing we can do tonight

3:15:33 in response to that. So,

3:15:36 yes.

3:15:39 Okay. Who's next?

3:17:47 protected, replanted where they need to

3:17:49 be replanted and additional steps will

3:17:52 be taken uh to manage this from an

3:17:57 obviously be a lot of uh transit and

3:17:59 traffic and parking impacts during the

3:18:03 covered by theou.

3:18:05 Now I'm not sure uh to what extent this

3:18:08 is also a public hearing on the question

3:18:10 of the hall street design which is also

3:18:13 on the agenda for this meeting as item

3:18:15 11b. Um, I attended the transportation

3:18:19 board uh meeting, I believe on February

3:18:21 25th, and there was a very productive

3:18:23 discussion of how to retain that U-turn,

3:18:27 inbound U-turn at Hall Street for the

3:18:30 benefit not only of the neighbors, but

3:18:31 for other users who uh needed to turn

3:18:35 around without having to go well out of

3:18:37 their way. Um, I came away encouraged

3:18:40 that the U-turn would be maintained.

3:18:43 It's right in my neighborhood and I hope

3:18:45 that that will be possible um through

3:18:48 staff work to come up with the design

3:18:50 that uh preserves trees, modernizes the

3:18:54 station, makes it ADA compliant and

3:18:56 keeps that um inbound uh U-turn in a

3:19:00 fairly functional way. Uh so those are

3:19:03 my only comments at this point. I've

3:19:05 spoken previously, but again, I would

3:19:07 like to thank everyone who's worked on

3:19:09 this for so long, and I hope that we can

3:19:12 still make a little progress on some of

3:19:15 the issues that the other speakers have

3:19:16 raised and nail everything down in theou

3:19:20 so that commitments are clear and

3:19:22 commitments are respected as this goes

3:19:25 forward. And I look forward to seeing

3:19:27 the conclusion of this project and the

3:19:30 final result. Thank you very much.

3:22:28 Okay.

3:22:30 Um, I see Brian Kaine had his hand

3:22:32 raised.

3:22:34 Please go go to the podium.

3:22:39 Thank you very much. Uh, Brian Kaine, I

3:22:41 have the great honor to chair the

3:22:42 transportation board and work with some

3:22:43 amazing uh, DPW transportation

3:22:46 officials. Um, and uh, due respect, uh,

3:22:50 you're pretty persuasive when you need

3:22:52 to be. Uh, and so I think a lot of the

3:22:55 um the concessions that were made are

3:22:58 due in large part to to to what you uh

3:23:01 yourself and Michael put forward, Paul.

3:23:03 So, thank you for that. Um, as as I

3:23:06 mentioned the last time we did this, um,

3:23:08 Brookline has won these negotiations and

3:23:11 it's really important that we not lose

3:23:13 sight of what this is at the end of the

3:23:15 day. This is an accessibility project. I

3:23:17 mentioned at the last transportation

3:23:19 board meeting that I actually wrote my

3:23:20 graduate school application about making

3:23:22 the sealine accessible and that was 20

3:23:24 years ago. I was with a group of folks

3:23:26 uh some of them were in wheelchairs,

3:23:28 some of them weren't and and we were

3:23:29 trying to get ourselves back from

3:23:30 Washington Square to their hotel. It was

3:23:32 a holiday in at the time. Not sure what

3:23:33 it's called now. And uh we had to wait

3:23:35 so long to individually crank up that

3:23:38 ramp for every single person that a

3:23:40 couple of the folks couldn't make it and

3:23:42 had some accidents. And it was it was

3:23:44 really robbed them of their dignity. And

3:23:46 um you know I I sort of decided then and

3:23:49 there that this is something that we

3:23:50 needed to do as a society. I had didn't

3:23:52 live in Brook light at the time. I I had

3:23:54 no idea I'd be doing this. But look at

3:23:56 the end of the day this is an

3:23:57 accessibility project. We're going to

3:23:58 make each of these stations accessible.

3:24:00 Moving towards having the tea being 100%

3:24:02 accessible uh hopefully in the next

3:24:04 decade. It it'll be a first for a major

3:24:06 transportation public transportation

3:24:08 system in the country. and the the the

3:24:10 steps that this town is going through to

3:24:12 to do its part are incredible and are

3:24:15 very important and I really am grateful

3:24:17 to each of you um on the dis tonight for

3:24:20 for doing that and and for taking the

3:24:22 time to do that. Um unfortunately I have

3:24:24 a 9:00 meeting for my day job so I I

3:24:27 won't be around for the next part. So

3:24:28 let me just say real quick about Haw

3:24:30 Street. You you you saw the language of

3:24:33 our vote. Um, we have every intention of

3:24:35 retaining the U-turn at Haw Street as

3:24:38 long as it is safe to do so. We have

3:24:40 directed staff to look at every single

3:24:42 traffic study and do everything that

3:24:44 they can they can think of in terms of

3:24:46 signaling, in terms of smart signaling,

3:24:47 and in terms of infrastructure to retain

3:24:49 that U-turn. And if it is possible to do

3:24:52 in a safe way, we will do it. That is

3:24:54 the intent. And I think the the vote

3:24:56 language is really clear on that. So,

3:24:58 that is a commitment I'm happy to make

3:25:00 tonight to you folks. we will retain it

3:25:02 if it is at all physically safely

3:25:04 possible to do so. Uh with that again uh

3:25:08 Paul just want to echo your comments.

3:25:10 This was a a long process but it was a

3:25:12 good process and I am very grateful to

3:25:14 the folks uh behind me from the tea. Uh

3:25:16 some of whom I see far too often in

3:25:18 other public meetings for this.

3:25:19 Brookline does things in a unique and

3:25:21 special way but I think we ultimately

3:25:23 get a better product for it and I think

3:25:25 this is a great example of that. So

3:25:27 thank you very much folks.

3:25:29 Thank you. We have another raised hand.

3:25:31 Okay.

3:25:32 Jocelyn Murphy.

3:25:36 Thank you. This is Jocelyn Murphy. I'm a

3:25:38 precinct 16 town meeting member and a

3:25:40 member of the advisory committee.

3:25:42 Speaking for myself,

3:25:44 um I I would like to reiterate it's very

3:25:46 difficult to hear. Um and of course if

3:25:49 turning big on doesn't really repair the

3:25:51 problem because then there's an echo. So

3:25:54 um it has been very hard to hear folks.

3:25:56 But I I really just want to echo and

3:26:00 support what Alisa Pongja and Cara and

3:26:03 Sean said about trees in general, but

3:26:07 also about nesting

3:26:10 um animals in particular um owls and

3:26:14 squirrels and the other creatures that

3:26:16 live in these trees. It's a tremendous

3:26:18 number of trees that are being removed.

3:26:20 I 100% support accessibility on the tea,

3:26:24 but my concern is that a number of

3:26:27 citizens have asked meeting after

3:26:29 meeting

3:26:31 whether the MBTA will take steps to

3:26:34 support the animals that are likely

3:26:36 nesting in these trees and really never
