3:26:40 disagree that there's nothing the select

3:26:42 board can do. You can, this is a public

3:26:45 hearing and there are MBTA folks here

3:26:47 and you can ask them what steps they

3:26:50 plan to take to support the wildlife

3:26:52 that reside in the trees that are about

3:26:55 to be or will be soon cut down. Thank

3:26:57 you.

3:27:06 Okay. Any any more?

3:27:08 Okay. And as I said, we will not as a

3:27:11 board uh make a commitment uh but

3:27:14 individual board members of course can

3:27:16 can ask questions and uh uh seek uh uh

3:27:20 answers from the MBTA or others. So

3:27:25 um

3:27:25 thank you chair. I I hope someone on the

3:27:27 board will ask that question.

3:27:28 Okay. Well, we'll see if switch over to

3:27:30 big so I can hear you. Thank you.

3:27:32 Someone will

3:27:35 uh Okay. Wait. Um, anyone else wanting

3:27:38 to speak? May I'll close I will close

3:27:42 the public hearing and uh u ask the

3:27:46 board if there are any further comments.

3:27:48 I see John had his hand up first. Yes.

3:27:52 Well, I think everybody heard the

3:27:53 question that Joselyn asked, so I put

3:27:55 that to our MBTA representatives. Uh,

3:27:58 what is your response to that question?

3:28:02 Well, there were two to fairness there

3:28:04 was two questions. Well, you you Paul,

3:28:07 you go ahead and enumerate them.

3:28:09 Well, so one was the the question about

3:28:10 wildlife and then there was also a

3:28:12 question about I think some other, you

3:28:14 know, green space that could be enhanced

3:28:16 and I I believe we probably have a plan

3:28:18 for that with some extra tree plantings

3:28:20 that we might be able to speak to. But

3:28:21 this this issue of nesting animals, has

3:28:24 this been brought to your attention? I

3:28:26 do you have a

3:28:27 We certainly saw we received a copy also

3:28:29 I believe the select board the the

3:28:31 petition that was mentioned uh as the

3:28:32 MBTA has stated and was stated here

3:28:34 tonight. We do follow all the

3:28:35 environmental regulations that we are

3:28:37 required to do. So we worked with MASDOT

3:28:39 and had an environmental biologist come

3:28:40 out, assess the situation, give us best

3:28:42 practices, tell us what we need to do,

3:28:44 and then we will also have um an

3:28:47 environmental individual from the MBTA

3:28:49 will be on site during all this. Now,

3:28:51 can the MBTA guarantee no animal will be

3:28:53 harmed in this? We absolutely cannot. I

3:28:55 think it's unreasonable to expect that.

3:28:56 In fact, I not to be facitious, but

3:28:58 honestly, people will say we need pest

3:29:00 control because of rats whenever we do

3:29:02 work. And so we treat and we know that

3:29:03 that has a tendency to harm wildlife

3:29:06 too. Birds eat the dead rats and then

3:29:07 that has a perpetual impact. So we're

3:29:09 being realistic here that we are going

3:29:11 to do construction but we have best

3:29:12 practices in place. We have treatments

3:29:14 that uh mitigate that that reduce those

3:29:16 impacts but we cannot make the

3:29:18 commitment that no wildlife will be

3:29:19 harmed but we can make the commitment

3:29:20 that we will work handinand with the

3:29:22 experts to do it the right way and do it

3:29:24 the safest way possible.

3:29:26 Right. Thank you.

3:29:27 The the shrubs I'm sorry I'm calling

3:29:29 them shrubs. I shouldn't jump to that

3:29:30 thing. But we have committed to the

3:29:32 town. we have uh funding available. If

3:29:34 those funds remain after doing the tree

3:29:36 planting along Beacon Street that was

3:29:38 described, we are happy to plant where

3:29:40 the the town directs us to do so. Okay,

3:29:43 Michael.

3:29:44 Um, just following up on the tree

3:29:47 question, um, I I know that there's

3:29:50 particular concern about nesting

3:29:52 periods, and I'm wondering whether you

3:29:54 can speak to the schedule, um, of the

3:29:58 projects to understand when the trees

3:30:01 would be coming down in the course of

3:30:03 the projects.

3:30:04 Yeah, it's our understanding and again,

3:30:06 the environmental experts have uh, let

3:30:07 us know that the springtime is the peak

3:30:09 nesting time. As I mentioned, we would

3:30:11 start in that late April period, but I

3:30:13 don't mean we're going into full

3:30:14 construction at that point in time.

3:30:15 We're starting to do some early work.

3:30:17 We're doing some site assessments. We're

3:30:18 doing some cings. We're figuring out

3:30:19 exactly everything that's going on. And

3:30:21 so, under their guidance, those trees

3:30:23 will not come down until later in the

3:30:24 project. Now, there may be some trees,

3:30:25 small trees without nest in them that

3:30:26 certainly can come down, but we will not

3:30:28 take those larger trees with those nest

3:30:30 until the environmental regulators tell

3:30:31 us that that is safe to do.

3:30:33 Thank you, Paul.

3:30:36 And I know the thanks for the answer.

3:30:38 That's very helpful. And I'm not an

3:30:40 environmental specialist. Neither are

3:30:43 you and I don't think they're here. But

3:30:44 do you have a sense at all about what

3:30:46 what is the the regulations or the

3:30:49 guidance with respect to let's say

3:30:50 there's a nesting sparrow uh in a tree

3:30:52 that you need need to take down? Would

3:30:54 you not take that tree down if there was

3:30:56 a nest? Do you wait until it's empty? Do

3:30:58 we do we have that understanding of the

3:31:00 details? I

3:31:01 I don't have those details. As I said,

3:31:02 they they have a a window and I think,

3:31:04 you know, they're trying to to solve a

3:31:06 very large complex problem by giving us

3:31:08 parameters that say that if you do it

3:31:10 within this zone, this window in this

3:31:13 time, you're highly unlikely to disturb

3:31:15 anything. And so that's what we target.

3:31:17 But we can't see every tree, every limb,

3:31:19 every branch, some nest are hidden. So

3:31:21 it is impractical. I'm sure that

3:31:23 certainly if we observed one, we would

3:31:24 do whatever we could. But the int but

3:31:26 the intent of of the guidance or the

3:31:29 regulations is to uh minimize or um you

3:31:34 know um not have harm happen.

3:31:36 Correct.

3:31:37 You're not just you're not just taking

3:31:38 the tree down without checking and Okay.

3:31:41 All right. Thank you,

3:31:43 John. Uh I'm curious about something and

3:31:45 I apologize for not having asked this

3:31:47 question earlier in the process, but I

3:31:50 visited uh those two stops uh just

3:31:53 within the past week or so and at each

3:31:55 stop where there is a stair there is an

3:31:58 MBTA there's a there's a lift for um to

3:32:03 make it handicapped accessible but it's

3:32:05 obvious that in each case the lift has

3:32:08 been neglected severely over the years

3:32:11 and is in you know complete state of

3:32:13 disuse and uh and probably need of total

3:32:17 replacement. Are lifts no longer

3:32:19 acceptable under the ADA as uh uh

3:32:24 providing access?

3:32:26 So those lifts uh actually came up in my

3:32:28 very first meeting when I started

3:32:29 working on this project and those are uh

3:32:31 actually town assets. So I don't have

3:32:33 any information about the lifts or when

3:32:35 they quit working, but you're correct.

3:32:36 They have not worked since I moved here

3:32:37 over seven years ago.

3:32:38 They're town assets.

3:32:40 Correct. As are the stairs.

3:32:42 Yeah. But no, the the those lifts, those

3:32:44 styles are not considered uh compliant

3:32:46 for ADA purposes simply because they do

3:32:48 fail too often. They break down. So

3:32:50 that's why the ramps are required.

3:32:52 Elevators are allowable, but elevators

3:32:54 are much more disruptive uh and would

3:32:56 require more significant construction

3:32:57 and tree loss than the ramps do.

3:32:59 Do you have any stations where there are

3:33:00 lifts these days?

3:33:02 No.

3:33:03 No. Okay. Interesting.

3:33:06 I mean, just in case people are

3:33:08 wondering about lifts, we've had one at

3:33:10 my church which has been usable for 20

3:33:12 years. Um, I'm not sure why, you know,

3:33:15 they can't be usable. I if they're not

3:33:17 comply if they don't if they don't

3:33:18 comply, I mean, they don't comply, but

3:33:21 um, you know, the these were not usable,

3:33:23 I assume, long before there was a change

3:33:25 in the compliance rules because u, you

3:33:27 know, they're just all rusted out and

3:33:29 you know, uh, and you know, m mechanical

3:33:33 junk at this point.

3:33:36 Okay,

3:33:37 Bernard. Sarah's raising her hand

3:33:39 online.

3:33:39 Okay, Sarah,

3:33:44 is she promoted?

3:33:46 Yes.

3:33:49 Hello. Um, I'm the town's ADA

3:33:52 coordinator, community relations

3:33:53 specialist. I'm also a person who uses a

3:34:02 not reliable.

3:34:04 and also very embarrassing to use.

3:34:08 People take pictures of me when I in

3:34:10 those lifts. They um and then also like

3:34:14 you have to train staff and new staff

3:34:16 come on all the time and people don't

3:34:18 know how to use them and is I've been

3:34:21 stuck in those lifts before. They're not

3:34:23 reliable ways to get on and off the tea.

3:34:28 Yeah. Thank you. Uh yeah, and and I I I

3:34:32 used if they're no longer sufficient for

3:38:37 board members um so just to go over the

3:38:40 transportation board directed us at

3:38:41 their last meeting um to look at the

3:38:45 Beacon Street Hod Street station

3:38:47 particularly the eastbound lane

3:38:49 configuration and they wanted us to um

3:38:52 make a commitment to maintain the U-turn

3:38:54 movement by looking at um these

3:38:57 configurations. And some of them may

3:38:59 include a Beacon Street Eastban has a

3:39:01 shared through U-turn lane and then one

3:39:04 through lane. Or another option could be

3:39:06 a Beacon Street exclusive um U-turn lane

3:39:09 and one through lane or maybe moving the

3:39:12 left turn lane to another intersection

3:39:14 like a Kent Street. And what we're going

3:39:16 to do is look at those options and then

3:39:19 um present back to the transportation

3:39:21 board in the neighborhood the um

3:39:23 operational changes and the impacts

3:39:25 these might have and we'll select the

3:39:27 preferred solution. And then just to

3:39:30 kind of outline what this looks like,

3:39:31 this is what the current proposed um

3:39:34 plan looks like. There's two through

3:39:36 lanes and the U-turn is restricted.

3:39:39 And then for the option, we're going to

3:39:41 look at if it's um a through and left

3:39:45 turn lane with a through lane.

3:39:48 And then another option could be an

3:39:50 exclusive turn lane, but only one

3:39:51 through lane.

3:39:53 And then this would be an option to

3:39:55 relocate a left turn at Kent and Powell.

3:42:40 item 11 C, memorandum of understanding

3:42:43 with the MBTA. The final item relates to

3:42:46 a memorandum of understanding between

3:42:48 the MBTA and the town of Brooklyn.

3:42:51 Because Beacon Street includes shared

3:42:53 right-of-way areas and overlapping

3:42:56 infrastructure responsibilities, the

3:42:58 town and the MBTA have been working to

3:43:01 clarify responsibilities related to

3:43:04 maintenance and operations along this

3:43:06 corridor. This agreement established

3:43:09 establishes clearer expectations

3:43:12 regarding responsibilities for trees and

3:43:15 landscaping,

3:43:16 street lights and parking meters,

3:43:19 sidewalks, and related infrastructure,

3:43:22 snow removal responsibilities at

3:43:24 stations, platforms, and within the

3:43:27 right away. The goal is to ensure that

3:43:30 both the MBTA and the town have clear

3:43:33 operational responsibilities and access

3:43:37 for maintenance moving forward. So any

3:43:40 uh discussion

3:43:43 of this item

3:43:46 that's going to be entered into

3:43:50 commissioner you want to talk a little

3:43:51 bit about thank you uh Erin Chute

3:43:54 Commissioner of Public Works. Um, so as

3:43:57 the board knows, the MBTA operates uh

3:44:00 the green line within the public's right

3:44:02 ofway under a grant of location and

3:44:04 areas around the stations and platforms

3:44:07 um where uh our responsibilities

3:44:09 naturally meet. Uh over the course of

3:44:12 this project, DPW staff have been

3:44:14 working closely with the MBTA's C branch

3:44:18 right of way and operations teams to

3:44:20 improve coordination and day-to-day

3:44:22 operations.

3:44:24 Theou is intended to capture those

3:44:26 working understandings in writing so

3:44:28 that both organizations and future staff

3:44:31 have clear guidance moving forward. At a

3:44:34 high level, the agreement will clarify

3:44:36 that the MBTA is responsible for

3:44:39 operation and maintenance of its

3:44:40 stations, platforms, track beds,

3:44:43 associated station infrastructure, while

3:44:45 the town remains responsible for the

3:44:47 roadway, sidewalks, drainage trees,

3:44:50 traffic infrastructure, and other

3:44:52 municipal assets, parking meters within

3:44:54 the public right ofway. Uh, one area

3:44:57 we're looking to uh clarify is snow

3:45:00 removal. uh snow operations occur

3:45:03 simultaneously across all MBTA and town

3:45:05 assets and the agreement will outline

3:45:08 which organization clears u platforms,

3:45:11 ramps, track crossings, public

3:45:13 sidewalks, roadway crossings and

3:45:15 connections to the broader pedestrian

3:45:17 network and bus stops. And the the

3:45:19 intent is to ensure that both

3:45:20 organizations can maintain their

3:45:22 infrastructure efficiently

3:45:24 and the public experiences a continuous

3:45:26 and safe accessible path during winter

3:45:29 operations. So theus is intended to me

3:45:32 memorialize certain operational

3:45:34 commitments also associated with the

3:45:36 construction of the accessible platforms

3:45:39 as that project moves forward. The

3:45:41 language of theou is currently being

3:45:44 refined by town staff working with town

3:45:46 council and the MBTA. What we're asking

3:45:49 the board to authorize this evening is

3:45:51 the ability for the town administrator

3:45:53 in consultation with DPW and town

3:45:55 council to finalize and execute that

3:45:58 operational agreements once the language

3:46:00 is complete. And this is really intended

3:46:03 to be an operational agreement between

3:46:05 the two organizations so that our staff

3:46:07 on both sides can continue to coordinate

3:46:10 efficiently and maintain both the town

3:46:12 and the MBTA infrastructure uh in a

3:46:15 clear and consistent way going forward.

3:46:17 There have been a lot of improvements in

3:46:19 communication, a lot of it brought about

3:46:22 because of this project. And so it's

3:46:26 really an opportunity for us to put that

3:46:28 in writing for clarity uh for the

3:46:31 future.

3:46:34 Great. Thank you. Any questions?

3:46:38 Are we ready to vote?

4:04:36 uh a few accomplishments from the

4:04:43 their roadmap um which is a series of

4:05:48 need to be complete. Um we'll be

4:05:50 wrapping up uh what was $40 million and

4:05:53 over 120 projects internal and external

4:05:56 um in ARPA funding. Um finally, we're

4:05:59 going to continue our efforts to

4:06:00 digitize and streamline the licensing

4:06:02 process, but I want to highlight here

4:06:03 that the road map itself discusses

4:06:06 looking for efficiencies. And so, not

4:06:08 only in the area of licensing, but I

4:06:10 think we'll be looking for other areas

4:06:12 we can create uh efficiencies and

4:06:14 streamline things for staff and

4:06:16 residents um and board and committee

4:06:18 members. So, there's just a few of the

4:06:21 of the um stats we're going to use to

4:06:23 track our our streamlining of licensing.

4:06:26 We just implemented those this year and

4:06:28 so hopefully next year we'll start

4:06:29 having actual data we can report out to

4:06:32 the board.

4:06:34 Continuing right along, the office has

4:06:37 nine total FTEEs. And what we're showing

4:06:39 you here on this pie chart is that we

4:06:41 really don't have any groups of

4:06:42 employees. We're mostly a a group of

4:06:44 several specialized folks that are each

4:06:47 doing each working in their own areas.

4:06:49 Um we're a very lean staff. Um we are

4:06:53 losing our ARPA funded grant

4:06:54 administrator at the end of FY27. Um so

4:06:58 no plans to change staffing at this

4:07:00 point. you know, referencing the

4:07:02 guidance that was sent out to

4:07:03 departments, we no plans to change

4:07:05 staffing at this point, but we are

4:07:06 losing that position. Um, and digitizing

4:07:09 licensing is one of those examples of

4:07:11 trying to provide efficiencies without

4:07:13 needing to add staff in order to achieve

4:07:16 those. So, that's something that that

4:07:17 the the guidance mentioned as well, you

4:07:20 know, talking about where we're we're

4:07:21 looking to create efficiencies. And we

4:07:23 think that we're we're making good

4:07:24 progress there.

4:07:27 And so, um, finally, um, not to breeze

4:07:31 through everything too quickly, uh, the

4:07:33 board asked about a couple of things or

4:07:35 perennially asked about a couple of

4:07:36 things, which was increasing demands and

4:07:38 what we would do without budget

4:07:39 constraints. And so, the the highest

4:07:42 increase in demand that we have seen is

4:07:44 really in fiscal oversight. The uh, the

4:07:47 last fiscal year close was was a

4:07:49 difficult one with a couple of

4:07:50 significant overtime deficits in public

4:07:52 safety. Um this year we have a

4:07:55 significant snow and ice deficit. We've

4:07:57 had the issue with the transfer station

4:07:59 floor. We're talking about potentially,

4:08:01 you know, we'll obviously have another

4:08:02 fire overtime deficit. Um and so these

4:08:05 these fiscal years are requiring more

4:08:07 and more um effort and work at least

4:08:09 from our end of the office, Melissa and

4:08:11 I's end of the office and monitoring

4:08:13 deficits, monitoring spending issues and

4:08:15 concerns. Um utilities are also a

4:08:18 concern this year. we're seeing, you

4:08:20 know, as a result of geopolitical

4:08:22 conflicts, rising costs. Um, and then

4:08:24 we're we're also getting this feedback

4:08:26 that, um, board and committee support is

4:08:28 an area that we're seeing increased

4:08:30 demand. Um, and and I I also list

4:08:33 communications here. This is partly ADA

4:08:35 compliance and and and partly community

4:08:38 outreach demand, but you know, more of

4:08:40 our time and maybe that is we're victims

4:08:42 of our own success in that in that um

4:08:44 situation, but more of our time is going

4:08:46 to to outreach and and communications.

4:08:50 So, uh without budget constraints, what

4:08:52 would we do in our office? Would our

4:08:54 priorities change? Um my sense is that

4:08:56 we wouldn't change our priorities, but

4:08:58 we could significantly expand our

4:09:00 capacity. You know, I mentioned earlier

4:09:01 that the department has several

4:09:03 specialized folks. Each of those, in my

4:09:06 opinion, could be its own division. You

4:09:07 could have a division of community

4:09:09 engagement. You could have a division

4:09:10 that's dedicated to finance, that's

4:09:12 dedicated to budget projections. Um, you

4:09:15 know, and of course, we would expand on

4:09:17 professional development. We could we

4:09:19 could really expand on what we do with

4:09:20 department heads on a quarterly basis to

4:09:22 provide training to the department and

4:09:24 division heads. And of course, we could

4:09:25 ramp up resident services to the to to

4:09:28 the levels I think that people really

4:09:30 idealistically would like to see. Um,

4:09:32 and so that is the select board's budget

4:09:35 for FY27. I want to take a moment to

4:09:59 add here, um please feel free. But I

4:10:04 and I will be glad to take any

4:10:06 questions.

4:10:10 the staff, the whole team. Um it's a

4:10:13 really great team. Uh we're we're

4:10:14 incredibly lucky to have all these

4:10:29 folks like Michelle too and and Kate who

4:16:17 Michael,

4:16:18 um thank you very much, Charlie. Um I

4:16:21 have a couple of questions. Um the first

4:16:23 question is um

4:16:26 given that we are losing the grant

4:16:28 administrator

4:16:29 um can you give us any sense of what the

4:16:32 grant revenue is that that person was

4:16:35 responsible for bringing in um so that

4:16:38 we understand sort of what the cost is

4:16:41 versus what the revenue is that that

4:16:43 person was generating.

4:16:45 Yeah, that that's a great question,

4:16:46 Michael. So this position was created in

4:16:48 order to administer the American Rescue

4:16:51 Plan Act. Sharpa funds um that came

4:16:54 through as a result of COVID right from

4:16:56 the federal government. So um that was a

4:16:59 you know 40 plus million I think 42 to

4:17:01 $43 million

4:17:03 um that they didn't necessarily bring in

4:17:05 we would have received it either way and

4:17:07 they their task was to ensure that that

4:17:09 money was spent correctly and to

4:17:11 basically act as the administrator of

4:17:13 the money for subreients. So t you know

4:17:16 um Tyler Balile in our office um who's

4:17:19 been excellent with ARPA um has I think

4:17:22 also served as a as a contact when

4:17:24 needed for questions around using you

4:17:26 know SAM accounts and federal you know

4:17:29 doing federal grant applications. I

4:17:31 can't say off the top of my head how

4:17:32 many additional grants Tyler actually

4:17:34 brought in if any. Um I think he's been

4:17:37 pretty busy with the 43 million in ARPA.

4:17:40 Now, with that said, um Michael, you

4:17:43 rais a good point and a good question.

4:17:45 Um you know, I've talked with Tyler a

4:17:47 lot about whether or not we should or

4:17:49 could have a grant position in town and

4:17:51 what that would look like or even just a

4:17:53 grants process because it's fairly

4:17:55 decentralized at this point and we think

4:17:57 there's capacity for it. Um but um but

4:18:01 unfortunately, yeah, Tyler himself

4:18:03 didn't bring any additional revenue over

4:18:05 or above AR ARPA. I just I just briefly

4:18:08 add to that. Um

4:18:10 um when we talked to the expenditures

4:18:11 and revenue study committee and we we

4:18:13 were asked you know we asked all the

4:18:14 departments if money was no object what

4:18:16 are your top three things? One of my top

4:18:18 three things was bringing on a permanent

4:18:20 grants administrator. Um and um the ra

4:18:24 the reason for that is as Charlie said

4:18:26 our process is decentralized. Some

4:18:27 departments are very good at hunting

4:18:29 down grants and funding themselves and

4:18:31 some of them don't have the capacity to

4:18:32 do it um and need a helping hand. I also

4:18:35 need to have insight into our overall

4:18:38 long range strategy for pursuing grants

4:18:40 and prioritizing them. You know, right

4:18:42 now I file all the earmark applications

4:18:45 for federal dollars myself, you know, I

4:18:47 ask the department heads, what do you

4:18:48 need? And then I stay up late and I put

4:18:49 them together. Um, you know, that's not

4:18:52 an efficient use of my time. Um, and

4:18:54 given the amount of money that people

4:18:56 could be getting for those, you know, if

4:18:58 we were if we were really strong at

4:19:01 developing that grant function, I think

4:19:02 it is a worthwhile investment in the

4:19:04 long run. But it's something that has to

4:19:06 come after this override cycle. Um,

4:19:09 again, it would be really nice and I

4:19:12 think it would ultimately be a money

4:19:13 maker for the town to have centralized

4:19:15 grant support operations. And I want to

4:19:17 be clear, you know, I'm not saying to

4:19:19 those departments who do good job. We're

4:19:21 going to take that function away from

4:19:22 them. We want to be able to coordinate

4:19:24 with them and ensure that we're all

4:19:25 everyone is is able to function at that

4:19:27 kind of high level. Um, but right now we

4:19:30 just we don't have the capacity. Um, and

4:19:33 you know, I don't want to be in a

4:19:34 position of saying, you know, I'd like

4:19:36 to add a staff, a permanent staff member

4:19:38 when we're asking other departments to

4:19:39 make cuts. So, that's where we are.

4:19:42 So, this financial plan is based on a no

4:19:45 override scenario.

4:19:47 Yes.

4:19:48 Correct.

4:19:49 If the override does pass, I don't

4:19:51 recall that this position is recovered.

4:19:54 No, it is not.

4:19:55 Though, as you said, uh, it it generates

4:19:58 a huge amount of money or could generate

4:20:00 a large amount of money. It could. Yeah,

4:20:04 and as always, you know, we we we don't

4:20:06 budget in soft money into the hard money

4:20:08 budget for for good reasons. Um, so it

4:20:10 is something that's that's difficult to

4:20:12 quantify and can be ephemeral in ways,

4:20:14 but we, you know, we obviously track our

4:20:16 grant funded things. We try and, you

4:20:18 know, make sure that people understand,

4:20:21 what's, you know, what's what's on the

4:20:23 what's in the operating budget versus

4:20:24 what's out of it. Um, we try and do all

4:20:27 of that and I think we're successful at

4:20:28 it. Um but again we think about the

4:20:31 priorities of the community, the

4:20:33 priorities that have been said to us in

4:20:34 expenditures and revenues. You know, our

4:20:36 goal to try and keep the town override

4:20:38 number at the same or lower than the ask

4:20:41 was in 2023 and the large amount of

4:20:45 needs in other departments. You know,

4:20:46 between, you know, fire, police, and

4:20:49 DPW, that's pretty much it. That's the

4:20:50 ball game. You know, a couple of other

4:20:52 departments are getting small numbers,

4:20:53 but you know, I was just, you know,

4:20:55 talking to the health director and kind

4:20:56 of lamenting, you know, there was space

4:20:59 in this override. Yes, I would give more

4:21:06 there. Um, you know, we we do what we

4:21:09 can with what we have.

4:25:45 now we have an ARPA funded agreement

4:25:47 with Capital Strategic Solutions who is

4:25:49 um helping us with PR, right? Um and so,

4:25:53 you know, you could you could easily

4:25:55 have additional staff in our department

4:25:56 whose job it is to work with Christina

4:25:58 on all the projects she's working on.

4:26:01 um and expand upon that. I think um

4:26:04 tenfold. I think that right now um we're

4:26:07 doing a lot with a little put it that

4:26:09 way. So, and I don't know if anybody

4:26:11 else wants to jump in here and throw me

4:26:12 a lifeline on.

4:26:15 I'll just say when when I came on board,

4:26:17 one of the things that we did was we

4:26:19 moved a communications officer position

4:26:21 out of ODICR into the select board

4:26:23 office. And the purpose of that was to

4:26:25 give to centralize that function and to

4:26:29 in the same way that we talk about

4:26:30 centralizing grant administration. Some

4:26:32 departments are very good at

4:26:32 communication. Some departments don't

4:26:34 have any communication at all. But when

4:26:36 we did that, we expressly said that a

4:26:38 part of that role that part of that job

4:26:40 is to work with ODICR to target outreach

4:26:43 and to target communication particularly

4:26:45 with harderto-reach parts of our

4:26:47 community. And that is some of the work

4:26:49 that Christine has been doing. And ODICR

4:26:51 has also been, you know, taking the lead

4:26:53 in terms of thinking about how do we

4:26:56 reach people who are disconnected from

4:26:59 the community but who live here and, you

4:27:02 know, rely on the and could benefit in

4:27:04 from the services that we provide. And

4:27:06 so that question is out there. You know,

4:27:08 we're always thinking about how we how

4:27:10 we communicate, not just broadly with

4:27:13 the general public, but specifically

4:27:15 with parts of the community that have

4:27:16 been unrepresented or are are otherwise

4:27:19 disconnected or or, you know, um not,

4:27:22 you know, don't feel themselves to be a

4:27:24 part of the community as it stands. So,

4:27:27 um there is a joint role for ODICR and

4:27:30 the communications officer position to

4:27:32 play in that targeted outreach. Um but I

4:27:35 think on the whole moving communications

4:27:37 into the select board office and having

4:27:39 that sit there has been a success in

4:27:43 terms of how we've been able to broaden

4:27:45 our messaging out to the public.

4:27:48 And the the U diversity office does its

4:27:51 own communication with respect to

4:27:52 various programs that that it uh

4:27:55 supports or runs. So

4:27:59 they're not out of the game in that

4:28:01 regard. They do a very good job of that.

4:28:03 Right. I'm I I I'm mostly asking this

4:28:05 question as an organizational structure

4:28:07 question, not a question of like are

4:28:10 these different uh departments doing

4:28:15 good work or important work or work that

4:28:16 needs to be done. It's just is the

4:28:19 organizational structure optimized for

4:28:21 getting all of this done in the right

4:28:23 percentages.

4:28:24 So um

4:28:27 OD

4:28:29 O DICR will be um on our is on our

4:28:33 agenda for later on. So those are

4:28:35 questions that we can raise with them.

4:29:48 I'll talk uh more softly up. Um, but uh

4:29:52 I'm Joe Callahan, town council. Uh, and

4:29:55 here's my budget presentation, and I'll

4:29:57 try to keep it short, but I'm happy to

4:29:58 answer any questions. So, we are uh you

4:30:02 see me frequently, but there's many of

4:30:04 us. There's seven other other of us in

4:30:06 town council's office, five attorneys,

4:30:08 and uh three support staff. Uh John, who

4:30:12 counts our FTEEs. Uh in years past we

4:30:14 had an FTE count of 8.7. Um that was an

4:30:18 addition of an investigator that has

4:30:20 since moved over to HR. That position

4:30:23 wasn't filled but the year that the

4:30:25 investigator went over to HR we added a

4:30:28 half an FTE which are paid interns. So

4:30:31 that makes up our office which is

4:30:32 usually about 10 people.

4:30:35 Uh this is a change in the budget from

4:30:37 last year over this year. um it

4:30:40 represents a 1% decrease and that's

4:30:42 again in a no override situation. Um the

4:30:45 big decrease or big change is $30,000

4:30:48 reduction in downside services. There's

4:30:51 a an increase in salaries, but that's

4:30:53 just steps of our uh employees who are

4:30:56 not at the top step and an increase in

4:30:58 our cost of uh computers.

4:31:02 So I want to talk about two of our

4:31:04 accomplishments. Um, this is along the

4:31:07 lines of how we measure our

4:31:10 accomplishments as opposed to, you know,

4:31:12 the town council's office doesn't

4:31:14 produce a building or, you know, produce

4:31:16 a comprehensive plan. But we do work

4:31:18 with those other departments who do

4:31:20 those things to make sure they're done

4:31:22 legally and, you know, as best we can.

4:31:25 But how but there's two metrics that I

4:31:27 try to track that are specifically

4:31:29 related to legal and that is our

4:31:30 spending on outside counsel. And you've

4:31:32 heard this in years past. This is a

4:31:34 comparison of the last 12 years. Um,

4:31:37 four the last four years are ones I'm

4:31:39 responsible for because I've now been

4:31:41 with Brookline since March of 2022. So,

4:31:44 four years. But we went back another

4:31:47 eight years to uh, you know, compare our

4:31:49 outside spending costs versus what uh,

4:31:52 we've been spending on outside council

4:31:54 since I got here. You heard examples,

4:31:57 but actually there's another example of

4:31:59 why we don't need outside counsel. For

4:32:02 example, you heard many times uh many

4:32:05 many many nights about the negotiations

4:32:07 that we've had with the MBTA.

4:32:10 Um in my 11 years at the MB at the uh

4:32:12 state, I worked on a lot of railroad

4:32:15 title case. Um in the three years I was

4:32:18 in private practice, I represented the

4:32:20 MBTA 15 years ago. I had several trials

4:32:24 over the state of railroad title. I'm

4:32:26 one of the few attorneys that know about

4:32:28 railroad title. So when Aaron came to me

4:32:30 and said the MDTA is telling her we have

4:32:34 to accept what they want to do on their

4:32:36 you know sea line I said well if it's

4:32:39 within their granted location they're

4:32:40 right but is everything within their

4:32:42 granted location and we realized the

4:32:44 grant of location hadn't changed since

4:32:46 the 19th century even though the MBTA

4:32:50 property did and that gave us the

4:32:51 leverage to negotiate thatou that Aaron

4:32:54 was talking about otherwise I can assure

4:32:57 you I represented a community before

4:32:59 that was in the MDK area. The MDA would

4:33:02 have treated us differently, but we had

4:33:06 something that they needed, an amended

4:33:08 grant of location, and we then were able

4:33:11 to use what they needed from us to get

4:33:13 what we wanted from them. And that's

4:33:15 theou that's concessions on trees.

4:33:18 That's a lot of concessions that the

4:33:19 MBTA, you guys understand, just agreed

4:33:22 to. But because of my experience with

4:33:25 railroad title, I didn't need to go to

4:33:27 outside council. Instead, what I did is

4:33:29 have Susan Harris in my office learn

4:33:32 about railroad title. I know about it.

4:33:34 I've worked on it multiple times, but I

4:33:36 passed on my knowledge to other

4:33:38 attorneys in the office. So, that's what

4:33:40 we're trying to do with um the money

4:33:43 that we get. So, instead of spending

4:33:46 money on outside lawyers, we increased

4:33:48 our subscription to West Law. We got a

4:33:50 sub a membership in the social law

4:33:53 library. So it's a it's a law library

4:33:55 where you can get resources that aren't

4:33:57 online. Uh if you're looking for the

4:33:59 ward commission reports from the 60s

4:34:01 that's not on Wesla but it is in social

4:34:03 law library. You heard about you know

4:34:05 needs for professional development. What

4:34:07 we did was increase the money that we

4:34:10 spent through bar associations through

4:34:12 mass um Massachusetts continuing legal

4:34:14 education. All my attorneys can get

4:34:17 training if they want it. If you look in

4:34:19 um fiscal year 24 and 25, that's

4:34:22 spending that we we had on an outside

4:34:25 lawyer to train our new labor and

4:34:27 employment lawyer. We spent about um

4:34:29 $10,000 for him to do 40 hours of

4:34:32 training. But what he did was power he

4:34:35 recorded PowerPoint presentations. So

4:34:37 when he was training Elena Ben David,

4:34:41 our new labor employment lawyer, Elena

4:34:44 has left in the past year. All of Jack

4:34:47 Collins's videos are now available for

4:34:50 Barry Freedelland who replaced the link.

4:34:52 So even though we spent money on an

4:34:54 outside council, we got that money back

4:34:57 by the way we were able to use that

4:34:59 outside council. The last uh blip on

4:35:02 this for outside council is um to hire

4:35:05 August and Epstein. So you uh you know

4:35:08 are familiar with our Comcast

4:35:09 negotiations that

4:35:13 we lost. Oops.

4:35:16 Okay, looks like we lost you,

4:35:18 Joe. We lost you there.

4:35:21 Oh, okay.

4:35:23 But, um,

4:35:23 there we go.

4:35:24 Okay. So, so this has been our history

4:35:28 with outside spending. So, what I also

4:35:31 want to talk about is another

4:35:32 accomplishment of ours, which is claim

4:35:34 handling. And we on average here about

4:35:38 one or claims about 100 personal injury

4:35:41 or property damage. a motor vehicle uh

4:35:44 case where a cruiser or a fire engine

4:35:46 hit a car, a pothole claim, where

4:35:49 someone slipped on a sidewalk or in a in

4:35:51 a town building. On average, that's

4:35:53 about $350,000 a year people are looking

4:35:56 from the town. Now, we've had a similar

4:35:59 number. This here was an eight-year look

4:36:01 back. Um we have similar number of

4:36:03 claims, although the amount of money has

4:36:05 increased, but we've also settled more

4:36:08 cases than denied more cases in years

4:36:11 past. but we pay out each year the same

4:36:14 amount. And how we're able to do that is

4:36:17 by settling for lower amounts of money.

4:36:20 We spend the time negotiating with

4:36:21 outside counsel on, you know, you're

4:36:24 looking for, you know, a lot of money.

4:36:26 We're willing to settle for a little

4:36:27 money. So, those are a lot of words.

4:36:29 Here are the numbers. Again, this is a

4:36:31 comparison of 15 quarters versus 16

4:36:33 quarters. So, the numbers should be

4:36:34 about 6% less. Um, but you know, we're

4:36:38 about the same number of claims. The

4:36:40 amount of money claimed in all those

4:36:42 claims has doubled in part because of

4:36:44 inflation 25% over these last eight

4:36:47 years. But we pay out less or you know

4:36:50 approximately 5% less.

4:36:52 But how we do that is we used to settle

4:36:55 about 25% of the amount claimed. Now we

4:36:58 settle 11% of what's claimed. So there's

4:37:00 a 44% reduction in how much we pay out

4:37:04 even though we pay out close to 20% more

4:37:07 claims. So this is the hard work that we

4:37:10 do when we get a claim in and we

4:37:12 negotiate it and protect the taxpayers

4:37:14 uh you know uh tax dollars and we don't

4:37:17 just pay what opposing council is

4:37:20 willing to take. So, those are, you

4:37:23 know, two accomplishments that I want

4:37:24 to, you know, talk about. But you may

4:37:26 say like, "Oh, wait a second. We pay

4:37:27 lots of money for, you know, you know,

4:37:29 million-dollar claims. Those are the

4:37:32 ones that you hear about, but you don't

4:37:34 hear about the hundreds that aren't, you

4:37:37 know, in the news." And and those, you

4:37:40 know, the ones that I just talked about

4:37:41 are ones that we can pay within our

4:37:42 $75,000 settlement line item. If we

4:37:45 can't pay that, then we have to go to

4:37:46 the liability.

4:37:48 And here's the uh you know again

4:37:51 eight-year look back what we paid in the

4:37:53 liability fund. Now Alustin wasn't in

4:37:57 paid out of the liability fund. It was

4:37:58 bonded. So Alustin got $8 million but

4:38:02 the to the taxpayer is more because we

4:38:05 bonded it. However, the last two uh

4:38:08 payments what we've done is paid them

4:38:10 out of the liability fund. So even

4:38:12 though one of them was $1.8 8 million

4:38:15 because of the way it's structured in

4:38:17 the time value of money that's only

4:38:18 really worth $1.5 million because we're

4:38:21 basically borrowing against future free

4:38:24 cash as opposed to going out into the

4:38:26 market and having to pay interest. And

4:38:28 that's the same case with the um the

4:38:30 case of the teacher sex abuse. So again,

4:38:32 it's being paid out over term of years

4:38:35 through the liability fund. So we don't

4:38:36 have to borrow it and pay interest.

4:38:38 We're essentially the interest is coming

4:38:40 from ourselves. So, those are the two

4:38:43 accomplishments I wanted to talk about.

4:38:45 Um, I'll give you, you know, just kind

4:38:47 of a snapshot of what pending claims are

4:38:50 we have. We have 36 at the moment. Last

4:38:52 year at this time, we had 37. And this

4:38:54 kind of spreads it out into which

4:38:56 courts. Uh, but the matters are, and

4:38:58 this is pretty typical, you know, our

4:39:00 our litigation case load, but in I can

4:39:03 assure you none of these cases pose any

4:39:05 significant potential for financial

4:39:07 liability. There's probably two that we

4:39:08 might be liable, but again, it's

4:39:10 unlikely. And it's unlikely that that'd

4:39:12 be more than you know a couple hundred

4:39:14 thousand dollars. So you're not going to

4:39:15 see something like you've seen in the

4:39:17 last two uh you know settlements that

4:39:20 we've had. So otherwise I'm happy to

4:39:22 answer any questions either now or

4:39:24 later. You guys know my number. I give

4:39:26 it out. 617308-1525.

4:39:29 That is my cell phone number. I could

4:45:02 So, like I said, we're the um office of

4:45:05 diversity, equity, and um inclusion and

4:45:07 community relations. Um

4:45:11 you know, we have a team of um four um

4:45:13 staff members. Um and I, uh before we

4:45:17 move forward, I just want to say that

4:45:19 we, you know, we have um a small staff

4:45:23 compromise of four uh four staff, but we

4:45:25 do have a small budget of $3,000

4:45:28 um for stipens for three interns per

4:45:30 year.

4:45:32 Um at this time I just want to

4:45:34 acknowledge the ODICR team. Um uh their

4:45:38 expertise uh expertise, dedication and

4:45:41 work ethic is highly valued by the

4:45:43 community and town staff. Um they're a

4:45:46 great smart team and we are very

4:45:48 fortunate to have them in the town's uh

4:45:50 workforce. I just want to take this time

4:45:53 just to thank them for their service.

4:45:59 Our office um promotes community uh

4:46:02 driven events and activities that aid

4:46:04 Brooklyn uh Brooklyn's community to

4:46:06 become more culturally competent and to

4:46:09 to be informed of the achievements and

4:46:11 barriers faced by our protected classes.

4:46:13 It continues to work with community

4:46:15 entities to examine quality of life

4:46:17 barriers due to racial inequities or

4:46:19 discrimination based on protected class

4:46:21 status and to help create strategies to

4:46:24 mitigate those be uh those barriers.

4:46:30 Uh we work with a very modest budget. Uh

4:46:33 as with other departments, we do have um

4:46:36 most of the funds goes towards

4:46:38 personnel. Um but the office this year

4:46:41 um the budget is decreased by $24,000

4:46:44 which is 5% u a decrease of 5% in the

4:46:48 fiscal year 2027.

4:46:50 Um the total reduction is compromised of

4:46:52 a $3,2657

4:46:56 increase in salaries to staff

4:46:57 compensation.

4:46:59 Um a $360

4:47:01 increase in lease uh computer equipment

4:47:04 and a reduction of $28,000 to the

4:47:07 professional tech services um a line

4:47:09 item. Um be specifically that's going to

4:47:12 be directed towards language access. Um,

4:47:15 currently staffing levels remained at at

4:47:17 3.72 FTEES.

4:47:25 Accomplishments. Um, overall, I just

4:47:27 want to note that we work with town

4:47:29 departments and community organizations

4:47:31 in their endeavors to promote and uh and

4:47:34 provide resources to the Brooklyn

4:47:36 community, including marginalized

4:47:38 groups. Um some of the highlights here

4:47:41 um you know collecting collected and

5:11:36 health, um, the financial plan had an

5:11:40 increase of almost $6 million projected.

5:11:43 Uh, the GIC has come out with new rates.

5:11:48 rate sheets from the GIC and, um, they

5:11:51 are slightly better than what we had uh,

5:11:53 estimated in the budget. We had

5:11:58 closer to eight. Um some of the plans

5:12:00 vary though, so we're waiting for those

5:12:02 final sheets. Um then we have our group

5:12:05 life budget which is going up by 3%. Our

5:12:08 Medicare coverage which is going up 4%

5:12:10 that's generally tied to wages. Our

5:12:12 pension uh funding is going up uh about

5:12:15 8% and that is based on our current

5:12:18 funding schedule. We will have a new

5:12:20 funding schedule probably sometime this

5:12:22 summer. Um we have a a schedule that

5:12:24 resets every two years. So we'll be

5:12:26 looking to see kind of based on market

5:12:28 performance and um kind of activity in

5:12:30 the fund uh what that might look like

5:12:32 and hope that we can still stick with

5:12:34 our 2030 timeline. Um and then the last

5:12:37 few groupings are uh transfers that we

5:12:39 make to certain trust funds. So we have

5:12:41 a trust fund for the public safety IOD

5:12:44 medical expenses. At this point the fund

5:12:46 has a healthy fund balance. So, we're

5:12:48 not recommending an appropriation uh

5:12:50 which is why you see the drop of

5:12:51 350,000.

5:12:53 We, as you all know, we are level

5:12:54 funding our contributions towards OPED.

5:12:56 So, that is uh flat for fiscal 27.

5:12:59 Unemployment compensation, we're

5:13:01 boosting by 500,000. I am keeping an eye

5:13:05 given the staffing reductions that might

5:13:12 do acknowledge that there likely would

5:13:14 be be some savings in group health that

5:13:16 likely would need to slide into

5:13:17 unemployment um in a no override

5:13:19 scenario. So we're we're keeping an eye

5:13:21 on both of those budgets. And then we

5:13:23 have a $200,000 increase in uh workers

5:13:26 compensation. Uh so for overall uh n

5:13:30 $9.5 million um obviously that will be

5:16:21 if the town is to have compensating

5:16:23 balance agreements, that is to say,

5:16:27 than we pay rather than the town

5:16:29 directly paying for services from those

5:16:31 banks, we would instead agree to uh keep

5:16:35 uh a minimum balance there. Um the law

5:16:39 requires that if the town is to have

5:16:41 those agreements, it needs to be

5:16:42 authorized annually by town meeting. Um

5:16:45 and so it would be good to have this

5:16:47 flexibility. We do not currently have

5:16:48 any compensating balance agreements, but

5:16:50 um it would be good to have this

5:16:52 flexibility particular continue to have

5:16:53 this flexibility continue particularly

5:17:44 Okay, next. Probably not as quick. Um,

5:17:46 article seven, establish an overrise,

5:17:49 I'm sorry, override stabilization fund.

5:17:52 Is that going to be you, Chaz?

5:17:54 Sure. Um, so as you know, one

5:17:57 possibility uh for a structure of an

5:17:59 override would be involving a situation

5:18:02 where you uh raise the tax levy and then

5:18:06 t and then tax more money than you spend

5:18:09 in a given year and put some of that

5:18:11 money aside in an override reserve fund.

5:18:13 And the purpose of that reserve fund

5:18:15 would be to cover operating deficits in

5:18:17 the out years of the override cycle. Um

5:18:20 the concept of this being that you would

5:18:23 if you a little more upfront, you have

5:18:26 to tax less over the course of the cycle

5:18:28 and then the assumption would be that by

5:18:30 the end of the cycle. Um there would be

5:18:33 changes in the financial uh situation of

5:18:35 the town such that there would be a more

5:18:38 it would be more advantageous to the

5:18:39 taxpayers to take uh a larger amount of

5:18:44 money uh up to take a larger tax

5:18:47 increase up front um but ultimately pay

5:18:49 less in taxes overall over the

5:18:51 three-year period. Now the expenditures

5:18:53 and revenue study committee is still

5:18:54 talking about this. It is not set in

5:18:57 stone that this is going to be part of

5:18:58 their recommendation to you and is not

5:19:00 set in stone that you have to uh move

5:19:02 forward on this at all. Um but in order

5:19:06 to give you the optionality that if

5:19:08 that's the decision you make in terms of

5:19:10 how an override is structured, we ask

5:19:12 that this question be put on the

5:19:14 warrant. Um if ultimately you decide not

5:19:16 to go in that direction, then you just

5:19:18 simply don't have to you don't have to

5:19:19 move it.

5:19:21 A question that was asked in

5:19:22 expenditures and revenues is can this

5:19:24 money be appropriated by anyone? You

5:19:26 know, is this a is you know are we

5:19:28 worried about this being a slush fund?

5:19:30 The answer is yes. It can be

5:19:32 appropriated for the purposes that it's

5:19:33 stated for. Um and so we want to be want

5:19:37 to have firm policies in place about how

5:19:39 we use this money and when. Um and but

5:19:42 that's part of the structure of any

5:19:44 given override. Uh if that's the

5:19:46 structure you decide to go with. The

5:19:48 other question we got was can the

5:19:49 purpose of the fund change? For example,

5:19:52 if things go better than expected and

5:19:54 there is a balance left in this account

5:19:56 at the end of the three-year override

5:19:58 can be spent for other purposes. And the

5:20:00 answer is yes. By a two-thirds vote of

5:20:02 town meeting, you can change the purpose

5:20:04 of the fund. Um or you can continue to

5:20:06 spend it on operating expenses. Um

5:20:08 because the way the fund is designated

5:20:10 is for operating expenses and because

5:20:13 those categories of money are fungeible.

5:20:15 for example, you could fund you you

5:20:17 could fund certain operating expenses

5:20:19 out of the fund and then use the savings

5:20:21 from that to fund a capital project, for

5:20:23 example. So, um it's it's easy to use

5:20:29 anything that's extra in the fund for a

5:20:31 different purpose, but we wouldn't be

5:20:34 we're not thinking about it in those

5:20:35 terms. We're thinking about it in terms

5:20:36 of stabilizing the during the three-year

5:20:40 period of any of any potential override

5:20:43 um so that the overall tax burden on the

5:20:45 voter is lessened.

5:20:48 Paul,

5:20:48 so is this so this is our article?

5:20:50 Yes.

5:24:53 expressing support for the Massachusetts

5:24:55 Medicare for all legislation.

5:24:58 Um,

5:24:59 is there someone?

5:25:01 Yes.

5:25:02 Yes. Oh, I'm online. I mean, I was um

5:25:06 there in person until about 20 minutes

5:25:09 ago. Um, so my name is Francis Lang

5:25:13 Laby. Uh this is my first time at um as

5:25:18 a petitioner at the select board and um

5:25:26 several other people were present um who

5:25:29 had to leave who were also in support of

5:25:32 this. I should say that this is a

5:25:35 resolution simply to put support for

5:25:39 Medicare for all on the ballot. Um,

5:25:44 so I will say that

5:25:48 the Wait, hold on just one second. I'm

5:25:50 so sorry.

5:25:54 The the United States is really

5:25:57 virtually the only Western country that

5:26:01 does not have a publicly funded

5:26:04 universal health care system. Um, it's

5:26:08 not a good distinction. And

5:26:11 Massachusetts has actually been in the

5:26:14 forefront of progressive

5:26:18 equitable movements in affordability and

5:26:21 health care.

5:26:24 Also, at this time, there's a whole

5:26:27 bunch of resolutions being um presented

5:26:32 in other towns in Massachusetts.

5:26:36 Having listened to a lot of the

5:26:39 discussion about um budget problems, I

5:26:44 want to say that um

5:26:48 Brooklyn would save it's been estimated

5:26:51 that through the um municipal calculator

5:26:54 and um with a lot of thinking about

5:26:56 this, Brooklyn would save about $30.5

5:27:01 million in 2027 with this um with

5:27:05 Medicare for all and there's a very

5:27:08 compelling study by the University of

5:27:10 Massachusetts which shows how much money

5:27:13 would be saved throughout the state. Um

5:27:18 it is not only however the savings that

5:27:21 Brookline and other towns would make.

5:27:24 I'm very aware of how many people with

5:27:30 our private health care system now

5:27:33 simply do not get health insurance. And

5:27:37 I'm thinking of young people in

5:27:39 particular who are locked into jobs

5:27:43 purely to get the health care benefits

5:27:47 um and are not able to do things that

5:27:50 would be valuable and that they want to

5:27:52 do. And this is true not only for young

5:27:54 people obviously um and

5:27:59 employers, including very much the town

5:28:02 of Brooklyn, um

5:28:05 spend huge amounts of money on health

5:28:09 care premiums for their employees. Money

5:28:13 that could be spent so much more um

5:28:16 beneficially in other ways. Um I'm not

5:28:20 an economist so I cannot go into detail

5:28:24 about the savings. Um but I do know a

5:28:28 number of pe many people including

5:28:31 myself um who are

5:28:35 not done well by by private insurance

5:28:39 companies. Anyway, I certainly think

5:28:42 that this should be put on the ballot.

5:28:44 It doesn't commit. It just that would

5:28:47 simply allow Brooklyn residents to vote

5:28:50 on it. And um really that's all I have

5:28:53 to say. I think it's kind of a nobrainer

5:28:57 to put this on the ballot. Um and I'm

5:29:00 sorry not to be there in person and I'm

5:29:02 sorry that the other individuals who

5:29:04 were going to be um speaking to this are

5:29:08 also not able to be here now.

5:29:12 Thank you.

5:29:59 There anyone who would like to speak? I

5:30:00 see Alec Levitz has his hand up and

5:30:03 Regina Crowley. Uh, so let's go with

5:30:05 Alec first.

5:30:07 Yes. Thank you very much, Chair Green.

5:30:08 And I'll try to be very brief. Um, I I I

5:30:11 agree with Francis and I I strongly

5:30:14 support this article. Um, as you heard

5:30:16 from Francis, um, Brookline would stand

5:30:19 to save substantially on healthcare

5:30:21 costs, um, should the state move to a

5:30:23 singlepayer model. Um, the financing

5:30:26 mechanism that has been proposed in the

5:30:28 legislation that this Warren article

5:30:29 endorses, um, would replace GIC rates

5:30:32 for Brookline with an 8% payroll tax on

5:30:36 our total salary line item. Um, as

5:30:39 Francis mentioned, that would save the

5:30:41 town, based on the FY27 financial plan,

5:30:44 $30.5 million in that fiscal year. We'd

5:30:47 be talking about going from spending

5:30:49 almost $50 million on group health

5:30:51 benefits um to slightly more than 17.

5:30:54 Um, as as we've heard earlier this

5:30:57 evening, and this is, I think, a very

5:30:58 opportune moment to take this article up

5:31:00 after having just heard the benefits

5:31:02 budget, um, healthcare cost increases

5:31:05 are are really reaching a crisis level

5:31:06 for municipalities. Um and I think it's

5:31:09 important to note that one of the

5:31:11 advantages of a singlepayer system in

5:31:12 addition to um providing savings through

5:31:15 just a change in the financing mechanism

5:31:18 for group health benefits would also be

5:31:20 that the state would have substantially

5:31:22 greater ability um to negotiate and

5:31:26 contain the cost of health care growth

5:31:28 over time. Um, and that would prevent

5:31:31 Brookline from facing single-year

5:31:33 healthc care cost shocks such as what

5:31:36 we've seen this year with a group health

5:31:37 rate increase of 14%. Um, and and I

5:31:41 think finally, as Francis also touched

5:31:42 on, uh, I I think this idea very much

5:31:45 fits within the community values of

5:31:47 Brookline and Massachusetts, uh, and I

5:31:50 think really seeks to continue

5:31:51 Massachusetts leading role in setting

5:31:54 healthcare policy across the country and

5:31:56 really envisioning how, um, concerted

5:31:59 state efforts can can drive um, national

5:32:02 health care policy conversations and

5:32:04 reform. Um, you know, we did that

5:32:07 already, uh, with the Affordable Care

5:32:08 Act. That model was pioneered in

5:32:10 Massachusetts and expanded nationally.

5:32:13 Um, and as to this date, no state has

5:32:15 implemented a state level singlepayer

5:32:17 system. Um, some have tried and failed.

5:32:20 U, no state has succeeded at this point.

5:32:23 And I think Massachusetts, for a number

5:32:24 of reasons, is uniquely situated to

5:32:27 succeed where some of those previous

5:32:28 efforts have failed. And I think the

5:32:30 implications for that in addition to

5:32:32 being a gamecher for our municipal

5:32:35 budget um and for the residents of the

5:32:37 Commonwealth, many of whom are either

5:32:38 uninsured or underinsured um it also has

5:32:41 the potential to drive a really critical

5:32:43 national healthcare conversation

5:32:44 forward. Um so I'm a strong supporter of

5:32:46 this Warren article. Uh I thank Francis

5:32:49 for bringing it forward and I would

5:32:51 encourage uh the select board to

5:32:52 consider voting favorable action. Thank

5:32:54 you.

5:32:56 Okay, Regina.

5:33:02 Thank you, Regina Frolley from precin

5:35:24 Did I close this? I'm sorry. I guess I

5:35:26 didn't close the hearing.

5:35:28 Let me just um say one additional thing.

5:35:32 I appreciate what the woman just asked

5:35:35 and what she brought up and I had

5:35:37 anticipated that this might be a

5:35:40 question and actually Alec um sent me a

5:35:44 very useful email in response and if if

5:35:47 he's still here he could speak to this.

5:35:50 Um yes, just very briefly what I had

5:35:52 referenced was the the main finance

5:35:55 mechanism in the bill that this warrant

5:35:57 article would endorse um is replacing

5:36:00 what is currently municipal direct

5:36:03 contributions from Brookline to the GIC

5:36:06 um with an assessed 8% tax on our total

5:36:10 salary line item. So essentially a 7%

5:36:13 state payroll tax. Um while that may

5:36:17 sound like a lot or excuse me, it's

5:36:19 actually an 8% tax. Um, that may sound

5:36:21 like a lot in a vacuum, but as I

5:36:23 mentioned, that actually represents a

5:36:25 substantial reduction in the town's

5:36:27 requested contribution um, for group

5:36:30 health benefits. And Regina is right.

5:36:33 Some costs will shift through the

5:36:35 economy. That is true. That's part of, I

5:36:38 think, um, the point of the healthc care

5:36:40 financing reform that's trying to be

5:36:42 advanced in this bill. Um but I think

5:36:44 the important thing to emphasize with

5:36:46 along with that is that the total cost

5:36:49 of health care um will be reduced and

5:36:52 the counterfactual relative difference

5:36:55 in how much health care cost will grow

5:36:57 over time will reduce will make the

5:36:59 reduction even more substantial. Um, so

5:37:02 while who pays what amount may shift

5:37:05 somewhat, most people including small

5:37:08 businesses, including municipalities,

5:37:10 and I would argue I believe most

5:37:12 residents um will pay less overall. So I

5:40:21 um so I one one thing I I do want to

5:40:24 stress and and you know I I work in

5:40:26 health policy so happy to get wonky on

5:40:27 this if people are interested but um a

5:40:30 singlepayer state level system will not

5:40:32 actually be Medicare for all because

5:40:34 Medicare for all is a federally

5:40:35 administered pro or Medicare rather

5:40:37 excuse me is a federally administered

5:40:39 program. The state has no ability to

5:40:41 directly administer Medicare. how the

5:40:44 states can have some influence um over

5:40:47 how Medicare is administered and also

5:40:49 how Medicaid is administered on the

5:40:52 state level is through um the

5:40:54 negotiation of waiverss with the federal

5:40:56 government. Um that allows states to to

5:41:00 customize some of these programs and how

5:41:02 they operate within state borders. Um

5:41:05 what has been a staple of previous

5:41:08 proposals and um Vermont's uh attempted

5:41:12 move to a state level singlepayer system

5:41:14 has been um alongside

5:41:17 the establishment of a state level

5:41:19 financing system, what Michael alluded

5:41:21 to, um and a state level um insurance

5:41:25 coverage system that covers non-Medicare

5:41:28 eligible people and people who are

5:41:30 currently on Medicaid. Um, it typically

5:41:33 involves trying to use your waiver

5:41:35 authority to customize the way Medicare

5:41:38 and Medicaid are implemented in

5:41:40 Massachusetts to fill in the gaps

5:41:43 essentially for where we would need

5:41:45 federal support. Um so even though uh

5:41:49 you know uh we would ensure say dual

5:41:51 eligible seniors could receive coverage

5:41:54 under a state singlepayer system, if you

5:41:57 only currently receive Medicare

5:41:59 coverage, um it's highly likely that you

5:42:02 would just continue to receive Medicare

5:42:04 coverage with potentially the state

5:42:06 offering supplemental coverage um to

5:42:08 address the coverage gaps in Medicare

5:42:10 that you referenced earlier, Bernard.

5:42:12 Thank you. Uh, John.

5:50:33 I thought it would be helpful just to

5:50:34 sort of recap what brought us to this

5:50:36 moment. Um back in

5:50:40 February 3rd, Economic Development

5:50:42 Division presented our recommendations

5:50:43 for the Chestnut Hill commercial area

5:50:45 study, which is a study that's been

5:50:47 underway since 2024.

5:50:49 Um, and included amongst those

5:50:52 recommendations was a proposal for um an

5:50:54 overlay zoning district. Uh, the select

5:50:57 board opted to hold a public hearing

5:50:58 regarding those recommendations on

5:51:00 February 10th. On February 24th, we were

5:51:03 given direction from the select board to

5:51:05 move forward with um submitting for the

5:51:07 select board's consideration uh a

5:51:09 package of warrant articles addressing

5:51:11 the overlay zoning as well as a package

5:51:13 of protections in the form of a

5:51:15 memorandum of agreement and tax

5:51:17 certainty agreement with city realy the

5:51:19 owner of the Chesn Hill office park at

5:51:20 1280 and 13:30 Boilston Street. So that

5:51:24 is what we have um submitted this

5:51:26 evening. There are the two warrant

5:51:27 articles. There's the set of proposed

5:51:29 zoning bylaw amendments to incorporate

5:51:31 the overlay districts, the Chesnut Hill

5:51:33 commercial overlay district. Um, as well

5:51:35 as a separate warrant article that would

5:51:37 authorize the select board to enter into

5:51:39 an MOA and tax certainty agreement with

5:51:41 City Realy. Um, so updates to the zoning

5:51:44 since uh the you saw the draft zoning in

5:51:47 your packet for February 3rd, there has

5:51:49 been some adjustments um primarily in

5:51:53 response to conversations we had with

5:51:54 the planning board. We had a

5:51:56 presentation and then a workshop with

5:51:58 the planning board and then a follow-up

5:51:59 discussion. So, we've incorporated some

5:52:01 of those changes that they requested in

5:52:03 terms of setbacks and height limitations

5:52:05 in certain areas um of the district. And

5:52:09 um we have also removed four properties

5:52:11 that are on the national register from

5:52:13 the eastern most part of um the

5:52:15 district. So, along Dunster Road. Um so,

5:52:18 the study boundaries have changed

5:52:20 slightly on the eastern side. There were

5:52:22 a couple of other follow items follow-up

5:52:24 items and requests that were um brought

5:52:27 up at the February 3rd meeting. Uh one

5:52:29 regarding sort of the transportation

5:52:31 board having the opportunity to weigh in

5:52:33 on the anticipated traffic impacts

5:52:35 associated with this upzoning. So we did

5:52:39 receive the future capacity analysis

5:52:41 from our transportation consultant. The

5:52:43 TA board is going to be reviewing that.

5:52:45 They're going to receive a presentation

5:52:46 from the consultant and then uh some

5:52:48 feedback from our transportation

5:52:50 engineering division staff with sort of

5:52:51 their technical assessment of um KDson's

5:52:54 work and recommendations and that's

5:52:56 going to be at their March 18th meeting

5:52:58 next week. And then finally, there was

5:53:01 uh the request that uh we circle back

5:53:04 with Newton. We've been in touch with

5:53:06 our counterparts in the Newton planning

5:53:07 department as well as the transportation

5:53:10 um planning staff regarding the study

5:53:13 process. However, with the benefit of

5:53:15 the study recommendations finalized and

5:53:18 um us finalizing the zoning, uh we have

5:53:20 been in more recent communication just

5:53:22 to update them on where things have

5:53:23 landed. Um and then, you know, giving

5:53:25 them the talking points that they need

5:53:27 to answer questions from Newton city

5:53:29 councilors and and leadership. Um we

5:53:31 also participated in a meeting with town

5:53:33 administrator Jazz Kerry and uh the

5:53:35 Newton mayor um mayor Laredo regarding

5:53:38 this proposal as well. Um, so that's

5:53:42 sort of our our update and where things

5:53:43 stand.

5:53:44 Just to follow up on that and thank you,

5:53:48 the planning staff and I went out went

5:59:06 challenge of the moment um is that I

5:59:08 went back and looked actually at the

5:59:10 MBTA Communities Act um process um and

5:59:14 one of the things that I noticed was

5:59:16 that the agreement that was that you

5:59:19 ultimately shephered through um to uh 83

5:59:23 84% % of town meetings vote uh actually

5:59:27 occurred about less than 30 days around

5:59:30 30 days from the time that the

5:59:35 um that town meeting began. Um and it

5:59:38 was a set of amendments to

5:59:42 to the warrant articles that were

5:59:44 ultimately filed. Um and um as I have

5:59:48 said uh repeatedly um that I am

5:59:54 uh still hopeful um and I believe that

5:59:57 there are trade-offs and negotiations to

5:59:59 be considered um and am uh looking

6:00:03 forward to those conversations. Um I

6:00:06 want to respect the multiple years of

6:00:10 effort um that have been put together by

6:00:13 uh the planning department um the

6:00:15 community advisory group um the number

6:00:18 of uh other stakeholders who have been

6:00:20 involved um and as you and I discussed

6:00:24 uh yesterday um this process is in some

6:00:29 ways more complex than other processes

6:00:32 because we have not only the town and

6:00:35 the community and the neighborhoods and

6:00:36 people who who are expressing those set

6:00:39 of interests. But we are also concerned

6:00:42 about uh the economic viability and

6:00:45 because we have a project in place um we

6:00:50 have more uh

6:00:54 more present uh uh expressions of what

6:00:58 would be economically viable or not. And

6:01:01 it is my hope that over the course of

6:01:03 this um examination that those

6:01:07 considerations of economic viability um

6:01:10 are continue to be reviewed and examined

6:01:13 and uh thoroughly vetted. Um but um

6:01:17 first of all I want to thank Meredith, I

6:01:20 want to thank um Carara, the rest of the

6:01:22 staff um Mike Sandman and the rest of

6:01:27 their continued engagement and work on

6:01:29 this and raising a number of critical

6:01:31 questions that have continued to be uh

6:01:35 incorporated into this body of work. Um

6:01:38 I'm very uh excited about this. I think

6:01:42 it represents both a vision um for the

6:01:45 entire study area as well as a project

6:01:47 which will deliver significant benefits

6:01:50 to the town um as I have mentioned in

6:01:53 the past um and I am ready to take the

6:01:57 next steps and continue to move forward

6:01:59 with all parties engaged.

6:02:45 in incorporates many many other

6:03:39 uh on to the east side of Tully Street

6:03:43 and he happens to have an interest in

6:03:46 possibly purchasing some uh residential

6:03:48 properties.

6:03:49 that are adjacent to his property on the

6:03:55 a position of um from his p perspective

6:04:23 have to be met for him to be able to do

6:04:29 to do. but he he kind of feels like he's

6:04:35 um no one seems to be interested in

6:04:38 paving the way for him to do a

6:04:40 development and um you know I'm not

6:04:43 going to judge whether that's an

6:04:45 accurate description. That's his

6:04:46 description of what's going on here. But

6:04:50 um as soon as we open the door to the

6:04:53 possibility of there being more than one

6:04:56 developer who would like to do

6:04:57 development um in this district, I mean

6:05:00 first of all that's kind of what we

6:05:01 wanted to happen. So you would think we

6:05:04 would do everything we can to encourage

6:05:06 this person. Um and then second of all,

6:05:09 you have to then begin a discussion

6:05:11 which we haven't yet yet had, which is

6:05:16 how much development can this whole area

6:05:19 sustain.

6:05:20 And if we're only talking to one

6:05:22 developer about one part of the uh

6:05:25 reszoned area

6:05:28 and if that developer um uses up a lot

6:05:31 of the capacity to absorb excuse me

6:05:34 additional units of well let's say

6:05:36 additional units of housing a whole

6:05:38 bunch of units of housing, a hotel, some

6:05:40 some retail, some office. Um uh it

6:05:57 happening at one end of this whole area

6:06:06 want to do their thing and they might

6:06:11 development if if for example there has

6:06:18 development.

7:06:06 public with the public for the public.

7:06:08 Thank you.

7:06:11 Thank you. Okay, with that, the meeting

7:06:13 is over.
